issue_title: Built-in XSS protection
issue_body:
In Misaka 1.0 there was a `SAFELINK` flag that provided a decent protection against XSS attacks, but it seems to have disappeared from upstream (<https://github.com/hoedown/hoedown/issues/209>).

Misaka 2.0 could fill the gap by providing a renderer subclass with custom `autolink`, `image`, and `link` methods that check URLs. Here's the one I've written: [markdown.py](https://github.com/liberapay/liberapay.com/blob/157/liberapay/utils/markdown.py). If I submit a Pull Request to integrate that class into Misaka, will you merge it?

issue_title: AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'which'
issue_body:
Hello,

I have tried installing dRep by first installing python3 from homebrew, then downloading pip, then downloading mummer and mash and scikit-learn (not this straightforward but ultimately got to this point upon troubleshooting things I seemed to need). 

First I was getting this error at the end of trying to install:
`error: Installed distribution numpy 1.8.0rc1 conflicts with requirement numpy>=1.9.0`

But I believe I overcame this by instead of just using '`python setup.py install`' but by linking to the python in my bin that had the numpy:
`bash-3.2# /usr/bin/python setup.py install`
and it seemed to install successfully. However I also tried to install numpy to python3 since it seemed to be missing by:
`karissas-mbp:~ ikf$ brew install numpy --with-python3 --without-python`

NOW, however, when I try 
`karissas-mbp:dRep ikf$ dRep bonus testDir --check_dependencies`
or 
`karissas-mbp:tests ikf$ python test_suite.py `
I get the same error:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):

issue_title: possible issue: gdbgui may freeze with very large files
issue_body: gdbgui may slow and exit if the binary file has a large number of source files. Output from the command `-file-list-exec-source-files` is very verbose, and parser-intensive. It might be a good idea to not run that command if the binary to debug is very large, or just just add a button that refreshes the source file list. The line in question is: https://github.com/cs01/gdbgui/blob/master/gdbgui/static/js/gdbgui.js#L1638

issue_title: Retry being called despite successful execution of function
issue_body:
On using `retry_unless_exception_type` , retry is always called (despite a successful run) until the exception(s) is(are) encountered. However, correct behavior with this condition should be to retry **only if a function has failed due to some exception other than the ones mentioned** in the retry condition.
e.g. This is the function definition, wherein I want to retry the function only if the exception is **not** among `psycopg2.ProgrammingError` and `NameError` and either the time since last retrial is less than 1 min, or the attempt count is less than 3:
```
@retry(stop=(stop_after_delay(60) | stop_after_attempt(2)),
        reraise=True,
        retry=retry_unless_exception_type((psycopg2.ProgrammingError, NameError)))
def run_query(query_variable):
    cursor.execute(query_variable)
    print cursor.rowcount
    print "Done"
```

and here's the execution log, which shows that function was retried twice despite successful runs:
```
53059
Done
53059
Done
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "update_offer_base_item_mapping.py", line 22, in <module>
    run_query(a)
  File "/Users/grofers/Documents/cb/py2venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/tenacity/__init__.py", line 241, in wrapped_f
    return self.call(f, *args, **kw)
  File "/Users/grofers/Documents/cb/py2venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/tenacity/__init__.py", line 330, in call

tenacity.RetryError: RetryError[<Future at 0x109cf4190 state=finished returned NoneType>]
```

issue_title: settings overwriting eachother
issue_body:
I have the following lines in my pylama.ini file
[pylama:*/**init**.py]
ignore = W0611

[pylama:bla/**init**.py]
ignore = E402,W0611

issue_title: Not receiving UNIX_FD property
issue_body:
This looks similar to #42 but the suggested modifications by @LEW21 do not seem to be having an effect. However I am right at the edge of my knowledge so apologies if this is a duplicate.

I am looking to implement the BlueZ Profile DBus APi 
https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/bluetooth/bluez.git/tree/doc/profile-api.txt#n104

I have implemented, published and registered the `NewConnection` method
``` python
class Profile(object):
    """
      <node>
        <interface name='org.bluez.Profile1'>
          <method name='Release'>
          </method>
          <method name='NewConnection'>
            <arg type='o' name='path' direction='in'/>
            <arg type='h' name='fd' direction='in'/>
            <arg type='a{sv}' name='properties' direction='in'/>
          </method>
          <method name='RequestDisconnection'>
            <arg type='o' name='path' direction='in'/>
          </method>
        </interface>
      </node>
    """

    def NewConnection(self, path, fd, properties):
        print('New connection', path, type(fd), properties)
        print('fd: ', fd)
        print('Is a tty? ', os.isatty(fd))
        print('tty name: ', os.ttyname(fd))
```
When a new connection is made the the Bluetooth daemon calls the `NewConnection` method but the value of fd is wrong:
```
New connection /org/bluez/hci0/dev_64_BC_0C_F6_22_F8 <class 'int'> {}
fd:  0
Is a tty?  True
tty name:  /dev/pts/0
```
I say wrong because it gives the tty of the terminal I'm running in and If I monitor the bus then I can see the bluetoothd sending the command with UNIX_FD = 4
```
linaro@linaro-alip:~/python/pydbus/pydbus$ sudo busctl monitor org.bluez
‣ Type=method_call  Endian=l  Flags=0  Version=1  Priority=0 Cookie=67
  Sender=:1.120  Destination=:1.132  Path=/ukBaz/bluezero/spp  Interface=org.bluez.Profile1  Member=NewConnection

issue_body:
I am trying to split subtiltes where there are two speakers that show up in the same frame. In my case this is indicated by newlines and hyphens ('\n-'). I this code snippet to split the subtitles into multiple:

`                for sub in subs:
                        if '\n-' in sub.text:
                                # Split the subtitle at the hyphen and format the list
                                lines = [line[1:] if line[0] == '-' else line for line in sub.text.split('\n-')]
                                length_milli = 1000 * float(sub.end.seconds - sub.start.seconds) + float(sub.end.milliseconds - sub.start.milliseconds)
                                interval_milli = int(length_milli / len(lines))
                                dummy = pysrt.SubRipItem(0, start=sub.start, end=sub.end, text="") # Use this just to get the right formatting for the time
                                print 'SUB'
                                print sub.start, sub.end
                                print 'DUMMY'
                                print dummy.start, dummy.end
                                dummy.shift(milliseconds =+ interval_milli) # Shift the dummy so its start time is now the end time we want
                                print dummy.start, dummy.end
                                for i in xrange(len(lines)):
                                        print 'NEW'
                                        new_sub = pysrt.SubRipItem(0, start=sub.start, end=dummy.start, text=lines[i])
                                        print new_sub.start, new_sub.end
                                        new_sub.shift(milliseconds =+ (i * interval_milli))
                                        print new_sub.start, new_sub.end
                                        subs.append(new_sub)
`
The basic gist is that to format the time I am using a dummy object so that I can take advantage of shifting. For example, a 3-phrase frame over 3 seconds is split 3 ways would be 1 second long for each new frame.

When I create the dummy as above using `start=sub.start` and `end=sub.end` and then shift the dummy, it also shifts the original subtitle. I suspect this was not the intended behavior.

I found that casting `sub.start` and `sub.end` to strings in the assignment (e.g. `start=str(sub.start)`) solved the issue. It appears that without the cast, however, I am actually assigning a reference or  pointer of some kind rather than the value of the string.

issue_title: Need an efficient way to communicate between curio tasks and other threads
issue_body:
Following up on some discussion in #111:

A standard feature in event loops is some sort of `call_soon_threadsafe` call, which allows code running in another thread to schedule a callback onto the event loop. This is then used as a primitive to build various sorts of communication mechanisms between code running in the event loop and code running in the thread. The standard way of implementing this involves using a wake-up pipe as a safe cross-thread notification mechanism.

For curio, we also need to be able do this kind of communication, though it probably makes more sense to have some sort of queue instead of a `call_soon` API, because `call_soon` is a [task-spawning API, and curio tries to avoid task-spawning APIs](https://vorpus.org/blog/some-thoughts-on-asynchronous-api-design-in-a-post-asyncawait-world/#c-c-c-c-causality-breaker).

Right now, the way to do this is to use a regular `queue.Queue`, and on the curio side access it via `await run_in_thread(queue.Queue.get)` (or the equivalent sugar: `abide(queue.Queue.get)`). Under the hood, `run_in_thread` relies on the `future_wait` trap to detect when the `get` has finished, and `future_wait` uses a wake-up pipe mechanism.

So that's all fine, but it's inefficient: every task that wants to communicate with a worker thread has to commit a whole extra thread just to sitting around waiting for the `queue.Queue`'s internal `threading.Condition` variable to get signaled. If we use `run_in_thread`/`abide`, then these threads all live in the same fixed-size thread pool, so we might even starve out other users of `run_in_thread` or get deadlocks. (E.g. if our thread pool has N threads, and we spawn N workers, and each worker needs to send back a message, and each message needs a thread, then we need 2*N threads total, and if all the workers fill up the thread pool and then block waiting for messages to be sent and responded to, then the whole thing will deadlock.)

The solution is to provide some more direct exposure of the underlying wake-a-task-from-another-thread primitive, which could be used to build a `Queue` that can communicate between curio tasks and other threads without using the thread pool.

issue_title: DNS object always has an 'empty' string data field
issue_body:
The dns decoder currently uses the data field but at the end sets it to an empty string.

```
    def unpack(self, buf):
        dpkt.Packet.unpack(self, buf)
        off = self.__hdr_len__
        cnt = self.qd  # FIXME: This relies on this being properly set somewhere else
        self.qd = []
        for _ in range(cnt):
            q, off = self.unpack_q(buf, off)
            self.qd.append(q)
        for x in ('an', 'ns', 'ar'):
            cnt = getattr(self, x, 0)
            setattr(self, x, [])
            for _ in range(cnt):
                rr, off = self.unpack_rr(buf, off)
                getattr(self, x).append(rr)
        self.data = ''
```

So looks like two options to me:
1) It's fine, leave it
2) We should remove the data attribute

Happy to do any of the above. @kbandla @obormot can pick one :)

Any idea how to get this working?

issue_title: dlib directly includes avx2intrin.h
issue_body:
Note: The following report is based on observations on ubuntu trusty using gcc 4.8.4.

dlib includes `avx2intrin.h` in [`simd/simd_check.h`](https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/bb0ac566db00bc24e3948829431891542977d582/dlib/simd/simd_check.h#L76) if it is compiled with avx2 enabled.
Our project includes multiple libraries, specifically dlib and opencv 2.4 (the latter also makes use of `immintrin.h`). The compiler flags contain `-march=native -O3` which leads to `__AVX2__` being set (compiling on a newer i7 CPU) and dlib's check enables `DLIB_HAVE_AVX2` as expected.

In this case I get error messages like this:
```
/usr/lib/gcc/x86_64-linux-gnu/4.8/include/avx2intrin.h: In function ‘__m256i _mm256_mpsadbw_epu8(__m256i, __m256i, int)’:
/usr/lib/gcc/x86_64-linux-gnu/4.8/include/avx2intrin.h:34:1: error: redefinition of ‘__m256i _mm256_mpsadbw_epu8(__m256i, __m256i, int)’
 _mm256_mpsadbw_epu8 (__m256i __X, __m256i __Y, const int __M)
```

In `/usr/lib/gcc/x86_64-linux-gnu/4.8/include/avx2intrin.h` the following check can be found:
```
#ifndef _IMMINTRIN_H_INCLUDED
# error "Never use <avx2intrin.h> directly; include <immintrin.h> instead."
#endif
```
This check is not triggered if `immintrin.h` was already included by another library.

To me it seems including `avx2intrin.h` directly is discouraged (at least in gcc 4.8.4). Maybe there is a special reason why dlib is including this file directly. If not, this would be an issue that could be fixed by changing [`simd/simd_check.h`](https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/bb0ac566db00bc24e3948829431891542977d582/dlib/simd/simd_check.h#L76) to not use `#include <avx2intrin.h>` but to use `#include <immintrin.h>`

issue_title: Error trying to use `cache_grammar`
issue_body:
Initializing a grammar object with the flag `cache_grammar` the following type error in Python 3.6:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "main.py", line 8, in <module>
    grammar = Grammar(f.read(), auto_filter_tokens=False, cache_grammar=True)
  File "/lib/python3.5/site-packages/plyplus/plyplus.py", line 550, in __init__
    plyplus_cache_filename = PLYPLUS_DIR + '/%s-%s-%s.plyplus' % (tab_filename, hashlib.sha256(grammar).hexdigest(), __version__)
TypeError: Unicode-objects must be encoded before hashing
```

Looks like this could be solved by simply adding a `.encode()` after grammar (at line [550](https://github.com/erezsh/plyplus/blob/61056e44f7efe94bf519feae420dd385ae6ca812/plyplus/plyplus.py#L550)).
But then pickle will complain about being unable to pickle the grammar (at line [558](https://github.com/erezsh/plyplus/blob/61056e44f7efe94bf519feae420dd385ae6ca812/plyplus/plyplus.py#L558)).

issue_title: Passing string to ask.get() with ;
issue_body:
Hi, 
I am trying to use asks for OSRM server requests.

I am sending a list of urls to my server.

Small example:

```

 async def main():
  r = await asks.get('http://35.204.249.209:5000/route/v1/driving/-73.62818000,40.75193800;-73.6356926,40.7418803?overview=simplified')
  print(r)
      
      
curio.run(main())
```
 

results in a bad request, because the OSRM server only gets 

`[info] 15-03-2018 15:54:39 0.064683ms 54.89.111.179 - python-asks/1.3.10 400 /route/v1/driving/-73.62818000,40.75193800?overview=simplified`

And if I replace the `;` with something else the OSRM gets the full URL (but of course its not working)

```
async def main():
  r = await asks.get('http://35.204.249.209:5000/route/v1/driving/-73.62818000,40.75193800%TEST-73.6356926,40.7418803?overview=simplified')
  print(r)
      
      
curio.run(main())

issue_title: asymmetric.generate_pair fails on Homebrew Python 2.7 + virtualenv
issue_body:
I bumped into a situation similar to #7, where calling `asymmetric.generate_pair` using Python 2.7 and virtualenv fails with `OSError: The user name or passphrase you entered is not correct.`. The twist is that I wasn't using the system Python 2.7, but one installed through [Homebrew](http://brew.sh/).

The issue can be reproduced by initializing and activating a new virtualenv using `python2.7` from Homebrew. It should be noted that in this case Homebrew files aren't in the usual /usr/local path, but inside the test user's home directory.

```sh
$ which python2.7
/Users/test/homebrew/bin/python2.7
$ python2.7 -m virtualenv test
$ source test/bin/activate
(test) $ pip2.7 install oscrypto
(test) $ python2.7
```
Now calling `asymmetric.generate_pair` in the virtualenv fails:

```python
>>> from oscrypto import asymmetric
>>> asymmetric.generate_pair("rsa", 4096)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  ...
OSError: The user name or passphrase you entered is not correct.
```

I suspect the problem is related to [lines 57-58 in oscrypto/asymmetric.py](https://github.com/wbond/oscrypto/blob/d5fa796fc792f08372c477c94b3d5eef9c564331/oscrypto/asymmetric.py#L57-L58) that check the `sys.real_prefix` path. In my scenario `real_prefix` is set, but doesn't begin with `_system_prefix`:

```python
>>> import sys
>>> sys.real_prefix
'/Users/test/homebrew/Cellar/python/2.7.12/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7'
```

issue_title: Exception - TypeError: datetime.datetime(...) is not JSON serializable
issue_body:
Some API calls in boto3 include `datetime` objects in the dictionary return values. Datetime is not one of the types that the `json` package understands, causing an exception when it tries to serialize it into a JSON file for the `save()` method:

```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/bin/testcode", line 9, in <module>
    load_entry_point('testcode==0.1', 'console_scripts', 'testcode')()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/testcode-0.1-py2.7.egg/testcode/main.py", line 511, in main
    user.save_placebo()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/testcode-0.1-py2.7.egg/testcode/main.py", line 228, in save_placebo
    self.client.meta.placebo.save(path)
  File "build/bdist.macosx-10.10-x86_64/egg/placebo.py", line 60, in save
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/__init__.py", line 189, in dump
    for chunk in iterable:
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 434, in _iterencode
    for chunk in _iterencode_dict(o, _current_indent_level):
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 408, in _iterencode_dict
    for chunk in chunks:
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 408, in _iterencode_dict
    for chunk in chunks:
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 332, in _iterencode_list
    for chunk in chunks:
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 332, in _iterencode_list
    for chunk in chunks:
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 408, in _iterencode_dict
    for chunk in chunks:
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 408, in _iterencode_dict
    for chunk in chunks:
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 442, in _iterencode
    o = _default(o)
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 184, in default
    raise TypeError(repr(o) + " is not JSON serializable")
TypeError: datetime.datetime(2015, 12, 3, 21, 20, 17, 326000, tzinfo=tzutc()) is not JSON serializable
```

The `json.dump()` method takes an optional argument `default`, allowing you to pass a function that returns serializable versions of objects. There's an example for datetime on Stack Overflow here:
http://stackoverflow.com/a/22238613

Similarly, the load() method will need to convert ISO 8601 strings back to `datetime` objects before using them for mocking. This could get tricky if someone uses timestamps for AWS object names, but maybe they could be marked for conversion. For example, `'placebo_timestamp:2015-12-3-21:20:17:326000Z'` is hopefully pretty unlikely as an AWS object name.

I will try to submit a PR for this soon.

issue_title: TG51 class PDD and TPR logic limits do not match documentation
issue_body:
The PDD and TPR limits specified in the documentation of the code do not match the program logic as specified in the code:

Inline documentation states:

```
pddx : {>=0.627, <=0.861}
    The *PHOTON-ONLY* PDD measurement at 10cm depth for a 10x10cm2 field.
tpr : {>=0.623, <=0.805}
    The TPR ratio of the 20cm measurement divided by the 10cm measurement.
```

The code logic implements the following correct values as limits:

```
PDD_LOW = 62.7
PDD_HIGH = 86.1
TPR_LOW = 0.623
TPR_HIGH = 0.805
```
But then seems to disregard the limits as the comparison does not include them, since it uses greater than and less than operators instead of greater or equal and less than or equal:
`if pddx > PDD_HIGH or pddx < PDD_LOW:` and `if tpr > TPR_HIGH or tpr < TPR_LOW:`

issue_title: Cassette path calculation from function outside of decorator?
issue_body:
[Updated] I would like to calculate the path name of cassettes from a function object as is done when used as a decorator but not using a decorator and retaining access to the built cassette.
What I've come up with is pretty gnarly but I don't see a better way to do it with the current code base (I'm using 1.12.0).

What I've got looks like this:

```python
# conftest.py

sample_vcr = vcr.VCR(
    path_transformer=compose(vcr.VCR.ensure_suffix('.yaml'),
                                                    ensure_prefix('sample_')),
    func_path_generator=compose(strip_test, lambda f: f.__name__),
    cassette_library_dir=str(samples_path() / 'vcr')
)

@pytest.fixture
def vcr_cassette(request):
    f = request.function
    ccd = Cassette.use_arg_getter(partial(sample_vcr.get_merged_config,
                                                                     record_mode='all'))
    fargs_getter = ccd._build_args_getter_for_decorator(f)
    ccd2 = Cassette.use_arg_getter(fargs_getter)
    with ccd2 as cassette:
        yield cassette
```

```python
# test_vcr.py

def test_vcr_fixture(vcr_cassette):
    assert vcr_cassette is not None
    assert hasattr(vcr_cassette, 'responses')
    import requests
    assert requests.get('http://httpbin.org/headers')
    assert vcr_cassette._path.endswith('sample_vcr_fixture.yaml')
```

This works, but as you can see, I have to create two instances of `CassetteContextDecorator` and use the explicitly non-"public" method `_build_args_getter_for_decorator`.

It seems to me that the work done in `_build_args_getter_for_decorator` could be done at the end of `VCR.get_merged_config`, although I have not tested it:

```python
class VCR(object):
    ...
    def get_merged_config(self, **kwargs):
        ...
        path = kwargs.get('path')
        if path:
            merged_config['path'] = path
        return merged_config
```

to

```python
class VCR(object):
    ...
    def get_merged_config(self, **kwargs):
        ...
        path = kwargs.get('path')
        if path:
            merged_config['path'] = path
        else:
            function = kwargs.get('function')
            if function:
                name_generator = (merged_config.get('func_path_generator') or
                                  self.get_function_name)
                path = name_generator(function)
                kwargs['path'] = path

        return merged_config
```

(Plus a few other changes, of course.)

I want to do this because I am attempting to make a pytest fixture, rather than using the decorator.
Why bother? Mostly because I don't want to have to import the `vcr.VCR` instance from `conftest.py` (or add a local module for helpers).
Upon further consideration, there are several better reasons for making it a fixture:
* The fixture can be used by a class, module or globally and generate separate cassettes for every test function.
* A fixture explicitly used by a test function would expose the various properties of `Cassette` that are (or seem to be) provided to make assertions against: `all_played`, `play_count`, etc.
* A command-line parameter to change the recording mode would be easier with a fixture (probably).

Would there be any interest in pursuing this further or is my use-case too limited?

        config_commands is an iterable containing all of the configuration commands.

        Automatically exits/enters configuration mode.

        if config_commands is None:

issue_title: Add webhooks
issue_body:
Add webhooks for various events, like on request submission, when actions are performed, etc. The goal for this is to make life easier for external clients and to make extending built-in functionality easier. A couple of use cases:

* Test has a Jabber bot that alerts us when a new request is filed. It'd be nice if instead of polling the API, it got a notification from the app via a webhook when a request was done.
* Nothing happens at the moment when a user leaves a comment, which can be problematic as almost everything else has notifications about comments. Adding a webhook means we can have a notification in Jabber when the submitter leaves a comment, and possibly a way to send a message to the submitter when someone else leaves a comment.
* It might be possible to replace the custom killmail subclassing currently used to customize request handling by using a webhook on request submission. If a request doesn't meet the requirements, the request can be rejected or incomplete automatically.

issue_title: melody.freq_to_voicing returns single boolean if not a numpy array
issue_body:
[```melody.freq_to_voicing```](https://github.com/craffel/mir_eval/blob/master/mir_eval/melody.py#L144) is meant to take a numpy array. I accidentally discovered that if you instead give it a list of floats instead of a numpy array, it returns ```True```, rather than an array of booleans.

```
>>> np.array([440.0, 0.0, 0.0]) > 0
[True, False, False]
>>> [440.0, 0.0, 0.0] > 0
True
```
I definitely just used the function wrong, but it is a bit of a sinister error. I don't have any ideas for an ideal solution... maybe the function could first check that ```frequencies``` is indeed a numpy array and throw a ```ValueError``` if not? Any ideas? Happy to make a PR.

issue_title: Transcription metrics
issue_body:
I think it'd be awesome for ```mir_eval``` to have metrics for evaluating automatic transcription (i.e. comparing two sets of symbolic notes). There's a relatively large number of people working on this problem in MIR, so I think such a module would very much come in handy.

Since I need this for some of my own work (ehm ehm), I'm happy to take a first stab at this.

As far as existing tools there's Emilio Molina's [matlab framework](http://www.terasoft.com.tw/conf/ismir2014/proceedings/T102_298_Paper.pdf) which has a fairly extensive set of metrics in it. For a first iteration though I'd stick to a widely used subset of metrics as defined in [Bello, 2003](https://qmro.qmul.ac.uk/xmlui/handle/123456789/3803); [Ryynänen & Klapuri, 2008](http://dl.acm.org/citation.cfm?id=1403132); and [Salamon, 2013](http://www.justinsalamon.com/phd-thesis.html) (page 186).

issue_title: bedtool.tabix_intervals: Too many open files error
issue_body:
Hi,
I'm using `metaseq` (https://github.com/daler/metaseq)  and it fails with **Too many open files** error while parallel process large *.bed files. This issue is due to `bedtool.tabix_intervals()` method:
1. `metaseq` splits input in lots of chunks for parallel processing
2. each chunk is processed with `metaseq.filetype_adapters.__getitem__()` which gets intervals iterator using pybedtools
3. `bedtool.tabix_intervals()` opens `tbx` file, but never close it

I see two possible fixes:
1) Close tbx file in `tabix_intervals()`

```
def tabix_intervals(self, interval_or_string):
        #...
        tbx = pysam.TabixFile(self.fn)
        results = list(tbx.fetch(str(interval.chrom), interval.start, interval.stop))
        tbx.close()
        return BedTool(results)
```

But in this case BedTool will create intermediate tmp file for results (see #73) and `metaseq` should do not forget to clean up loads of such tmp files

2) The other way is return bedtool object and tbx file descriptor pair and let user to close tbx file after handling bedtool

```
def tabix_intervals(self, interval_or_string):
        #...

       return (BedTool(results), tbx)
```

It breaks code compatibility but doesn't create intermediate tmp files. For my own purposes I've quick fixed `pybedtools` and `metaseq` using this way

I think my both suggested ways have disadvantages and some other way should be found.

A user managed to identify this problem as happening when a global variable is referenced from two different functions using the `global` keyword, the variable is not defined in the global scope at all, and Jedi tries to complete methods from the object.

Reproduction with Python 2.7.3 and Jedi 0.9.0:

```Python
import jedi

issue_title: Jedi doesn't complete inside modules with a name equal to their containing package.
issue_body:
Say we have a project with the following structure:
```
$tree
.
├── a.py
└── b
    ├── __init__.py
    ├── b.py
    └── c.py
```

If we try to get completions from inside file b/b.py, jedi fails to do so.
For example, in the following test script we see in which situations we can get completions.

```
import sys
import jedi

sys.path.append("/tmp/test")

source = """
from a import A
from b.b import B
from b.c import C
A().
B().
C().
"""

fmt = "{:15} {:20} {:15}"
print(fmt.format('file', 'line to complete', 'len(jedi.Script(...).completions())'))
print()

for p in ('test.py', 'b/b.py', 'b/test.py'):
    for line_num in (5, 6, 7):
        line = source.splitlines()[line_num - 1]
        script = jedi.Script(source, line_num, len(line), '/tmp/test/' + p)
        print(fmt.format(p, line, len(script.completions())))
    print()
```

Which outputs:
```
file            line to complete     len(jedi.Script(...).completions())

test.py         A().                              22
test.py         B().                              22
test.py         C().                              22

b/b.py          A().                              22
b/b.py          B().                               0
b/b.py          C().                               0

b/test.py       A().                              22
b/test.py       B().                               0
b/test.py       C().                              22
```

As we can see, we can't get completions in module 'b/b.py' for other modules in package 'b'.
Additionaly, modules from package 'b' such as 'b/c.py' cant get completions for 'b/b.py'.

issue_title: code-generation for ternary operator
issue_body:
I had problems with operator precedence when generating code for the ternary operator, original code such as:
`(X == 0) ? (Y=1) : (Y=2)`
which became after parsing and code generation:
`X == 0 ? Y=1 : Y=2`
which causes a compilation error since last assignment has lower precedence, essentially becoming
`(X == 0 ? Y=1 : Y) = 2`
I fixed this in in c_generator.py by modding to:
`    def visit_TernaryOp(self, n):`
`        s  = '(' + self._visit_expr(n.cond) + ') ? '`
`        s += '(' + self._visit_expr(n.iftrue) + ') : '`
`        s += '(' + self._visit_expr(n.iffalse) + ')'`
`        return s
`

issue_title: CPU frequency
issue_body:
It's unlikely this can be done on all platforms and all the times as this kind of info is typically not standardized and varies depending on the hardware. Anyway, it **may** be nice to have a `psutil.cpu_freq()` function returning a list of named tuples for each CPU including current and nominal (max) frequency.

**API**

```
>>> psutil.cpu_freq()```
[scpufreq(current=821, nominal=1600),
 scpufreq(current=1200, nominal=1600)]
```

The returned list should be consistent with `psutil.cpu_count()` and `psutil.cpu_percent(percpu=True)` both in length and indexing.

> Just so you guys know probably within a week there will be a major real-time system change to Discord.
the /gateway call will need the auth token because gateways will be assigned around a consistent hash ring
If you conncet to the wrong gateway it will send you a redirect opcode with the URL to the correct one

its special cause gateways support resuming a session id
instead of identify you send a resume

opcode is 7

If you want to support resuming a session you need to keep the session_id in your websocket instance.. and also keep track of the 's'  variable aka seq on events (keep last one)

send opcode 6 with {'session_id': session_id, 'seq': seq}

's'

issue_title: Implement asynchronous send queue
issue_body: One big difference between our networking stack and the one in the java client is that we send requests immediately while the java client puts them on an internal queue (per connection) and tries to send pending requests during calls to poll() . I would like to investigate using the same approach.

issue_title: Consumer offset migration from 0.9.5 KafkaConsumer to 1.x KafkaConsumer
issue_body:
When using `KafkaConsumer` from kafka-python 0.9.5, the consumer offsets are stored in ZooKeeper.
In kafka-python 1.x the offsets are stored in Kafka and it does not pick the offsets up from ZooKeeper, but starts at the end of the topic. This may cause messages to be skipped if the offsets are not migrated. I have tested this with broker version 0.9. 

According to Kafka [FAQ](https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/FAQ#FAQ-HowdowemigratetocommittingoffsetstoKafka%28ratherthanZookeeper%29in0.8.2?) and [documentation](http://kafka.apache.org/documentation.html#offsetmigration) the migration is handled by setting `dual.commit.enabled=true` in the Java consumer temporarily to force the offset being copied from ZooKeeper to Kafka, then reconfiguring the consumers again to only commit to Kafka. As far as I can tell, kafka-python does not have such option.

What seems to work for us is a simple script that takes the offsets from ZooKeeper and commits them manually to Kafka using the new client, but this needs to be done with all consumers in the consumer group to be down at the same time.

Other options include adding dual commit support to python-kafka or creating new topics for new consumers (the latter requiring to reconfigure producers as well at the time of migration).

I miss a migration guide in the documentation so I'd like to contribute one. What are the recommended steps to upgrade consumers to use the new `KafkaConsumer` implementation?

You probably have to do this via the PyPI admin interface :( I've never used the PyPI admin interface, but it should probably be set with a multi-use [Project Url Field](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0345/#project-url-multiple-use) like so:

This should populate the `bugtrack_url` field in https://pypi.python.org/pypi/whitenoise/json -- which in turn lets @dependabot (which I'm not affiliated with, but we use) find that the repo is hosted on GitHub and can in turn find the CHANGELOG file for its automated PRs. This makes it easier for people that use dependabot to view your changelog, and thus reduces friction for us with regards to keeping up with your releases.

issue_title: Use a consistent data type for fields with a given format?
issue_body:
These lines:

```
                # we don't need to split single entries
                 if entry_num == 1 or ',' not in vals:
```

in `parser.py` do not seem ideal. The first part of the condition – if the type of the field is `Number=1` – makes complete sense: do not provide a list for something that is always length 1. But the second part seems inconsistent when `Number` > 1, or `R`/`A` etc. A list is provided at one position and a non-list at another position because of the incidental difference in calls. It also makes it harder to deal with from client code - you can't only know the format of the field you are interested in. Is there a strong reason to do this?

issue_title: Text Column strange behavior on order_by and filter operations
issue_body:
Hi, I wrote this piece of code to report this strange behavior

```python
class TestText(Model):
    txt_identity = Text(index=True, keygen=IDENTITY)
    txt_simple   = Text(index=True, keygen=SIMPLE)

txt1 = TestText(txt_identity='BLACK',  txt_simple='BLACK')
txt2 = TestText(txt_identity='BROWN',  txt_simple='BROWN')
txt3 = TestText(txt_identity='BRONZE', txt_simple='BRONZE')
txt4 = TestText(txt_identity='RED',    txt_simple='RED')
txt5 = TestText(txt_identity='BROWN',  txt_simple='BROWN')
txt6 = TestText(txt_identity='BLACK',  txt_simple='BLACK')
txt7 = TestText(txt_identity='BROWN',  txt_simple='BROWN')

session.commit()
```
The impossible task, thus the strange behavior, is to order/filter txt objects using either the Text(IDENTITY) column or the Text(SIMPLE) column
Let's start with txt_identity
```python
# Filtering
TestText.query.select('txt_identity', ff=_tuple_data_factory).filter(txt_identity='BROWN').all()
Out[19]: [('BROWN',), ('BROWN',), ('BROWN',)]

# Ordering
TestText.query.select('txt_identity', ff=_tuple_data_factory).order_by('txt_identity').all()
UserWarning: You are trying to order by a non-numeric column 'txt_identity'. Unless you have provided your own keygen or are using one of the sortable keygens: (SIMPLE, SIMPLE_CI,CASE_INSENSITIVE), this probably won't work the way you expect it to.'''
Out[20]: []
```
So with Text-IDENTITY column you can do filtering but order_by fails and the warning explains that I have to use SIMPLE keygen. Fine let's see how Text-SIMPLE works:
```python
#Ordering
TestText.query.select('txt_simple', ff=_tuple_data_factory).order_by('txt_simple').all()
Out[22]:  [('BLACK',), ('BLACK',), ('BRONZE',), ('BROWN',), ('BROWN',), ('BROWN',), ('RED',)]

# Filtering
TestText.query.select('txt_simple', ff=_tuple_data_factory).filter(txt_simple='BROWN').all()
Out[23]: []
```
Text column with SIMPLE keygen seems to work fine with order_by but it fails on filtering.
That means there is either a bug or from the user point of view it is not practical to define two Text column attributes in order to do filtering and/or order_by. 
What do you think ? I am currently using the Text(index=True, keygen=IDENTITY) to work with a simple enumerated type but I would like to order my class objects by such a type. Is there anything I can do in this case ?

issue_title: `lstat()` dominates in the case of small coverage samples
issue_body:
The hypothesis library recently added coverage-led fuzzing, in which it needs to run a very short test many times, while examining the coverage between each trial. This (currently) involves many calls to `coverage.Collector.save_data`, which in turn causes many calls to `realpath` (and thus `lstat`). [In the extreme case](https://gist.github.com/bukzor/3e80b1d387bf68cd36198e1402bde8df), `lstat()` ends up taking about 40% of the runtime.

Can you please help us design a remedy? Some alternatives that I can think of:

1.  add a cache to files.abs_file
2. replace [the call to abs_file](https://github.com/nedbat/coveragepy/blob/coverage-4.4.2/coverage/collector.py#L400) with a call to files.canonical_path, since it already has a cache
3. Delegate the filename-normalization responsibility from Collector to CoverageData, such that we can specialize CoverageData and fix this within our dependent library.

issue_title: Verification doesn't work when using public key encryption
issue_body:
I'm using the RS256 algorithm, but I expect this will fail when using any of the other asymmetrical algorithms.  When accessing `/auth/verify` with the token, I get the failure:

```
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sanic/app.py", line 556, in handle_request
    response = await response
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sanic_jwt/blueprint.py", line 102, in verify
    request, *args, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sanic_jwt/authentication.py", line 169, in verify
    payload = self._decode(token, verify=verify)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sanic_jwt/authentication.py", line 62, in _decode
    **kwargs
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/jwt/api_jwt.py", line 78, in decode
    jwt, key=key, algorithms=algorithms, options=options, **kwargs
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/jwt/api_jws.py", line 140, in decode
    key, algorithms)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/jwt/api_jws.py", line 206, in _verify_signature
    if not alg_obj.verify(signing_input, key, signature):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/jwt/algorithms.py", line 317, in verify
    key.verify(sig, msg, padding.PKCS1v15(), self.hash_alg())
AttributeError: '_RSAPrivateKey' object has no attribute 'verify'
```

From my reading, this is because you need to use the public key to verify the token, since it was signed with the private key.  But sanic-jwt doesn't have a way to set the public-key.  It just has SANIC_JWT_SECRET.

https://github.com/jpadilla/pyjwt/issues/81

issue_body:
Not sure if this a bug or not, but:
```python
>>> alg = boolean.BooleanAlgebra()
>>> alg.parse("A").get_symbols()
[Symbol('A')]
>>> alg.parse("~A").get_symbols()
[]
>>> alg.parse("~A|B").get_symbols()
[Symbol('B')]
>>> alg.parse("~(A|B)").get_symbols()
[Symbol('A'), Symbol('B')]
```

So the literal NOT(A) is not retrieved by get_symbols

issue_title: ^C Signal does not kill program.
Have this generate binary wheels with latest amalgamation and ship them to PyPI
issue_body:
I'm really interested in this project at the moment.

SQLite 3.25.0 is due to ship shortly and will add support for [window functions](
https://www.sqlite.org/draft/windowfunctions.html), which is really exciting - but the actual version of SQLite that is available in the standard library sqlite3 module is tied to the underlying operating system and is often very out of date (the Travis CI default build environment still exposes SQLite 3.8 for example!)

I would love to see this project published to PyPI with the latest stable version of the SQLite amalgamation compiles into it, in a binary wheel format such that it can be "pip install"ed on as many platforms as posssible.

I haven't worked with binary compiled wheels myself, but I would be happy to dig in and help figure out how best to do this. 
https://github.com/biopython/biopython/issues/1296 has some useful notes and 
https://github.com/pypa/manylinux looks relevant. I've done a fair amount of work with Travis CI recently and it can provide build environments for both Linux and OS X (I have no idea how windows would work).

issue_title: TypeError: obfuscation_machine() got an unexpected keyword argument 'count'
issue_body:
I had this very easy issue when using the following command:
pyminifier --obfuscate --pyz=test.pyz test.py


Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/bin/pyminifier", line 11, in <module>
    load_entry_point('pyminifier==2.1', 'console_scripts', 'pyminifier')()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/pyminifier/pyminifier.py", line 302, in main
    compression.zip_pack(pyz_file, options)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/pyminifier/compression.py", line 222, in zip_pack
    use_unicode=True, count=identifier_length
TypeError: obfuscation_machine() got an unexpected keyword argument 'count'


The fix is straightforward, I already tested it on my side.
In the file pyminifier/compression.py on line 230 just substitute:
count=identifier_length
with:
identifier_length=identifier_length

Probably the keyword argument name was not changed everywhere.

Hope this helps!

When browsing on Raspberry Pi, a single `ListChildren` request may take up to 25s.
Nothing like this can be observed when running on Ubuntu (64 bit). Additionally, the 25s occur quite often, so this looks suspiciously like an internal timeout. Results are as expected, and no error occurs.

Some quick prototyping shows that this doesn't seem to occur when calling `ListChildren` asynchronously. Probably that's why this cannot be observed when searching (which usually finishes well below 1s), but can be observed in directory/artist/album lookups.

Thias has to be investigated further, but currently it looks like performing all operations asynchronously seems to be the way to go (which also has other benefits, e.g. when doing incremental/batch browse/search).

issue_title: py3-cmd performance
issue_body:
so as noted in #896 running `py3-cmd` is slow

```
$ time py3-cmd click 2 volume_status

real	0m0.388s
user	0m0.341s
sys	0m0.034s
```

running the code directly - minor tweak so send_command() is run

```
$ time python3 py3status/command.py click 2 volume_status

real	0m0.104s
user	0m0.090s
sys	0m0.011s
```

interestingly python2 is much faster
```
$ time python2 py3status/command.py click 2 volume_status

real	0m0.063s
user	0m0.049s
sys	0m0.014s
```

So the problem is that setuptools is really bad and wraps python scripts like so
```
$ cat /usr/bin/py3-cmd 
#!/bin/python3
# EASY-INSTALL-ENTRY-SCRIPT: 'py3status','console_scripts','py3-cmd'
__requires__ = 'py3status'
import sys
from pkg_resources import load_entry_point

if __name__ == '__main__':
    sys.exit(
        load_entry_point('py3status', 'console_scripts', 'py3-cmd')()
    )
```

here `from pkg_resources import load_entry_point` takes ages.  I've been looking into the issue which is a known one but seems is not well understood or documented.  There are some solutions but they are all quite hacky.  Clearly this is a problem for us as the speed of this command is far too slow.  Setuptools is altogether too 'clever' for it's own good and insists on rewriting any python scripts to be dog slow.  Any solutions I've found continue to be sub-optimal.  It maybe that it is just easier to write `py3-cmd` directly as a shell script.

So anyhow I've made this issue as a holding point.  Really the best solution is to fix setuptools but this looks unlikely.  My understanding though untested is that if users install via a wheel in pip then they won't have this slowdown but as I use `python setup.py develop` I am directly effected and it is too sucky.

Putting the version in the package
issue_body:
The `setup.py` file contains the version information in the `setup()` function call.  It's possible to get the version into a package level version identifier like this:

    from pkg_resources import get_distribution
    __version__ = get_distribution("bacpypes").version

But that seems brittle because it shouldn't have to depend on the package begin installed.  The alternative is to follow the pattern of other Python packages that put their version information into the `__init__.py` file like this:

    __version__ = '1.2.3'

While this makes it more cumbersome to update to the next version because there are at least three instances of this file, it seems simpler.  To keep yet another instance from appearing in the `setup.py` file, that can be updated with a little extra code like this:

    init_py = open(os.path.join(source_folder, 'bacpypes', '__init__.py')).read()
    metadata = dict(re.findall("__([a-z]+)__ = '([^']+)'", init_py))

and the version is in metadata['version'].  Comments?

<!---
@huboard:{"order":76.0,"milestone_order":70,"custom_state":""}
-->

issue_title: calc_expected_status_length does not work
issue_body:
calc_expected_status_length is broken in two ways right now. 

1. URL_REGEXP only recognizes URLs at the start of a string, which is correct for is_url, but for calc_expected_status_length, all URLs should be detected, not just URLs at the start of the tweet. There should be a different URL_REGEXP for calc_expected_status_length without the start-of-string makers.
2. The URL regex has multiple groups, so findall returns a list of tuples, not strings. If there are matches, replaced_chars = len(''.join(match)) crashes, it should be replaced_chars = len(''.join(map(lambda x: x[0], match))) instead

issue_body:
Hi! I've implemented simple empty strategy following quickstart guide. For datafeed I've loaded to memory 1M simple lines (1 min - bars for 2 years for certain ticker).

When I run this _empty_ strategy on _preloaded_ data feed on my Lenovo T410s (core i5, 8 GB RAM, Win 10 x64), memory consumption lineary increases until it throws OOM at 2 GB limit after 5-10 minutes of work!!

Either I am doing something completely wrong, or there is some big performance problem there.

Here is my code. When I uncomment "if self.__index >= 100000:" line to limit data with 100K elements, it consumes smth like 200-300MB RAM and finishes in 1 minute (for me it also seems very slow).

```
class BackTraderFeed(backtrader.DataBase):
    __index = 0

    def __init__(self, bars):
        super().__init__()
        self.__bars = bars

    def _load(self):
        # if self.__index >= 100000:
        if self.__index >= len(self.__bars):

        dt, o, h, l, c, v = self.__bars[self.__index]
        self.lines.datetime[0] = dateintern.date2num(dt)
        self.lines.open[0] = o
        self.lines.high[0] = h
        self.lines.low[0] = l
        self.lines.close[0] = c
        self.lines.volume[0] = v
        self.lines.openinterest[0] = 0.0
        self.__index += 1

if __name__ == '__main__':

    logger.info('Starting Portfolio Value: %.2f', cerebro.broker.getvalue())
    cerebro.run(exactbars=1, runonce=True) # tried to experiment with perf params here, nothing helps
    logger.info('Final Portfolio Value: %.2f', cerebro.broker.getvalue())
```

IMHO an empty strategy on only 1M lines should work in several seconds (strategy has no operations - so cerebro has only to iterate) and consume smth like 1M x 100 bytes = 100 MB (alright, maybe 500 MB, but no more). My "bars" list is like 150-200mb large.

Please help me to solve these performance problems. Thank you!

issue_title: HTMLRenderer parsing AutoLink as HTMLSpan
issue_body:
Broken in mistletoe 0.5.

The cause is that `HTMLSpan` is added in the first place of the token matching precedence. If we want `AutoLink` to work correctly, its precedence must be lower than `AutoLink`.

The difficulty fixing this is that even though `span_token.add_token` takes an optional `position` argument, it is called in `BaseRenderer.__init__()` and there's not a defined way for renderer subclasses to reach that. Plus it means increasing the complexity of `HTMLRenderer`, which, up to this point, has been serving as the example for custom renderer classes.

I'll have to think about it for a bit. Today is a great day for bugs it seems.

issue_title: YANGList type with string key containing a space char
issue_body:
For a YANG type containing a list which has a String Key, the Add and Get function expects a space delimited key string representation.

For example, a list which has a key of a String Value and an Integer value, the add function might expect:

`somelist.add("somekeystring 1")`

This logic breaks when the String key value contains a space

`somelist.add("some key string 1")`

On another note, is it possible for yangtypes.YANGListType.YANGList.add() to return a pointer to the newly created list object? Rather than the key value?

This shortens the following from

`somelist.add("somekeystring 1")`
`newitem = somelist.get("somekeystring 1")`

to be

`newitem = somelist.add("somekeystring 1")`

Thanks,
Mark

issue_title: Security risks of the default settings
issue_body:
The default `IPWARE_META_PRECEDENCE_ORDER` settings cause django-ipware to check for various types of headers by default. However, not all headers may be there, or get filtered through the webserver.

In a standard setup (apache+mod_wsgi or nginx+uwsgi), only `REMOTE_ADDR` applies and needs to be read. Since there are many other headers in `IPWARE_META_PRECEDENCE_ORDER`, I could pass any of these to fake an IP address:
* `X-Forwarded-For`
* `Client-IP`
* `X-Real-IP`
* `X-Forwarded`
* `X-Cluster-Client-IP`
* `Forwarded-For`
* `Forwarded`
* `Via`

I'd strongly recommend reducing `IPWARE_META_PRECEDENCE_ORDER` to just `REMOTE_ADDR` and let users configure their system explicitly when they use a forwarding http server in their setup.

issue_title: Allow varargs when using only keyword arguments
issue_body:
If a test function is wrapped by another decorator, it may be hard to avoid varargs. However, it should still be safe to use `@given` if no positional arguments are used.

I have experimented with this by modifying `run_test_with_generator` to do the following instead of the current vararg check:
```python
        if generator_arguments and original_argspec.varargs:
            raise InvalidArgument(
                'varargs are not supported with positional arguments to @given'
            )
```

`tox -e py26,py27` passes locally for me, so I have reasonable confidence that this doesn't break anything obvious. If you're okay with this change, I'm happy to submit a pull request with the necessary tests and such.

issue_title: Trap KeyboardInterrupt
issue_body:
Currently, if one creates a command in Python and one interrupts it with Ctrl+C, it prints a full traceback and exits with a status 1.

I'm inclined to think autocommand/automain should either always trap KeyboardInterrupt exceptions or offer an option/decorator to trap exceptions, e.g.:

```
@autocommand.autocommand(__name__)
@autocommand.trap_interrupt
def main():
    time.sleep(5)
```

As I think about it, other commands on interrupt will typically not render a traceback but will exit with a non-zero status.

```
$ sleep 1
^C
$ echo $?
130
```

So perhaps that's the functionality that autocommand.trap_interrupt should do.

I don't feel strongly about any of these ideas, but I thought I'd start the discussion to see what others recommend.

issue_title: [heaty] Add support for ordinary switches that are no climate entities
issue_body:
Heaty can be made compatible with entities of the switch component with only minimal effort.

To achieve that, the known thermostat settings ``opmode_service`` and ``opmode_service_attr`` will be split up into ``opmode_heat_service``, ``opmode_off_service``, ``opmode_heat_service_attr`` and ``opmode_off_service_attr``. The ``opmode_*_service_attr`` settings will allow a value of ``null``, in which case the operation mode won't be sent with the service call at all.

With these new settings in place, a switch entity could be configured as follows:

    switch.some_heater:
      supports_temps: false
      opmode_heat_service: switch/turn_on
      opmode_off_service: switch/turn_off
      opmode_heat_service_attr: null
      opmode_off_service_attr: null
      opmode_state_attr: state
      opmode_heat: "on"
      opmode_off: "off"

issue_title: needs to be made thread safe
issue_body:
I notice there are a number of places in watchdog that arn't properly thread safe. For example:

  EventDispatcher._dispatch_events()
        event, watch = event_queue.get(block=True, timeout=timeout)
        self.dispatch_event(event, watch)

This code path is not thread safe, as the watch object could get removed by the time the "dispatch_event" method obtains it's lock.

When running into this, it results in a KeyError and the resulting thread dying, thus watchdog no longer monitors a path. I had a traceback of this, but I've misplaced it at the moment, will past it it the next time I see it.

issue_title: Stop Parsing at Some Level or Exclude Tags
issue_body:
The issue we have is that current xmltodict parsing breaks the original text. For example:
Input:
<code>
<ABSTRACT>
Born: 22 July 1992 Selena <KW>Gomez</KW>
is an American actress and singer who has been working in the industry for quite some time, but is best known for her role as Alex
</ABSTRACT>
</code
so the "KW" is a markup tag for import words
Desired output is:
Key: "abstract"
Value: "Born: 22 July 1992 Selena <KW>Gomez</KW>
is an American actress and singer who has been working in the industry for quite some time, but is best known for her role as Alex"
Then we can do further processing as needed.

However, currently xmltodict gives this result:
(u'ABSTRACT', OrderedDict([(u'KW', u'Gomez'), ('#text', u'Born: 22 July 1992 Selena  is an American actress and singer who has been working in the industry for quite some time, but is best known for her role as Alex')]))
It extracts "Gomez" from the original text and we can not recover its original position in the text.

Is there a way to address this kind of issue?

Thanks,

issue_title: Dont blindly catch TypeError/AttributeError when parsing
issue_body:
Currently the library uses this code:

```python
    parser.buffer_text = True
    try:
        parser.ParseFile(xml_input)
    except (TypeError, AttributeError):
        parser.Parse(xml_input, True)
```

This is not great if you use callbacks which can raise a TypeError or AttributeError. In this case the original exception is swallowed and another unrelated one is raised (due to the `Parse` call).

Please can this be fixed?

issue_title: Add html5 push state middleware
issue_body:
It looks like the hashbang middleware checks for _escaped_fragment_ but the html5 push state section of the escaped fragment protocol says that URLs like:

`http://www.example.com/user/1` with the `<meta name="fragment" content="!">` meta tag will be accessed like:

`http://www.example.com/user/1?_escaped_fragment_=`

The current hashbang middleware might catch that case just fine but I had someone ask a question where it wasn't working because they took the hashbang middleware hour since they used html5 push state.

If the hashbang middleware would work for this case, then maybe that middleware should just be changed to escapedfragment or something instead of hashbang.

Thanks!

issue_title: Constructing a digraph with a vertex of large degree is slow
issue_body:
from pygraph.classes.digraph import digraph.

Adding edge to a graph is linear from the current degree of the vertex.
That's because ([link](https://github.com/pmatiello/python-graph/blob/master/core/pygraph/classes/digraph.py#L168) ; the check if the vertex is on node_neighbors) works in linear time.

That's because:
[node_neighbours](https://github.com/pmatiello/python-graph/blob/master/core/pygraph/classes/digraph.py#L136) is a list, not a set

I suggest 2 solution:
- separate method that adds several edges with the same end-point
- changing node_neighbours ( and possibly other containers; node_incidence) from a list to a set

issue_title: Issue: __get_result incorrectly overwrites the correct version when installed on python 3.
issue_body:
https://github.com/agronholm/pythonfutures/blob/5edbc65401fd578e195c2f0e2e8d3a6b5404f6bc/concurrent/futures/_base.py#L405-L416

I'm happy to make a PR to fix this, but I think the real solution is to release a new version that does not include the `python_requires='>=2.6, <3',`  and raises an error instead of a warning when attempted to be installed on python 3. 

For example, the monkey patching will break `pytest` if installed on `python3` with version `3.1.1`. That means if someone does `pip3 install -U futures`, that `python3` executable would install a `futures==3.1.1` because it doesn't have a `python_requires='>=2.6, <3',` which is what stops `pip3` from even seeing that `futures 3.2.0` exists. 

If we were to raise an error in a way analogous to what we do in IPython:
https://github.com/ipython/ipython/blob/01bd59ec7c184171df0cb0d933c5672e8c20b67e/setup.py#L25-L58:
```python
if sys.version_info < (3, 4):
    pip_message = 'This may be due to an out of date pip. Make sure you have pip >= 9.0.1.'
    try:
        import pip
        pip_version = tuple([int(x) for x in pip.__version__.split('.')[:3]])
        if pip_version < (9, 0, 1) :
            pip_message = 'Your pip version is out of date, please install pip >= 9.0.1. '\
            'pip {} detected.'.format(pip.__version__)
        else:
            # pip is new enough - it must be something else
            pip_message = ''
    except Exception:
        pass


    error = """
IPython 7.0+ supports Python 3.4 and above.
When using Python 2.7, please install IPython 5.x LTS Long Term Support version.
Python 3.3 was supported up to IPython 6.x.
See IPython `README.rst` file for more information:
    https://github.com/ipython/ipython/blob/master/README.rst
Python {py} detected.
{pip}
""".format(py=sys.version_info, pip=pip_message )

    print(error, file=sys.stderr)
    sys.exit(1)
```
then `pip install -U futures` would successfully avoid breaking peoples' `python3` executables by not allowing itself to be installed. I'll happiliy make a PR for this.

issue_title: Jenkins plugin doesn't work properly with mama-cas, developer suggests it might be mama-cas bug
issue_body:
Currently, the jenkins CAS plugin doesn't work with mama-cas when in SAML 1.1 mode (CAS 2.0 on the other hand works fine). Jenkins plugin bug ticket: https://issues.jenkins-ci.org/browse/JENKINS-28568

The jenkins plugin appears to be using this TARGET parameter (visible as GET parameter) for the /login endpoint. However, according to https://github.com/Jasig/cas/blob/master/cas-server-documentation/protocol/CAS-Protocol-Specification.md in section 2.1.1, the /login endpoint only knows the "service" parameter, but not explicitly the TARGET parameter.

The SAML 1.1 specification section 4.1.1.3 (https://www.oasis-open.org/committees/download.php/3405/oasis-sstc-saml-bindings-1.1.pdf) specifies such a generic TARGET parameter for any sort of SAML request. The plugin developer also tells us that (unlike mama-cas) the Jasig/Apereo CAS server interprets this parameter the same way as the "service" one.

Either mama-cas or the jenkins plugin seem to be not fully operating to the specs, although I certainly cannot tell with my limited knowledge. Therefore it would be nice if you could figure out which side needs to fix things so this can finally be resolved in whatever way is the best.

issue_title: OrderedDict problem with Python 3.5
issue_body:
When using cms-cascade with Python 3.5, the `SegmentPlugin.get_form` call fails due to return type changes in `OrderedDict.values`, which does not return a list anymore but instead an odict_values object. This object does not support index lookups anymore and thus needs to be converted to a list before accessing its items by index.

The incompatible lines are here: https://github.com/jrief/djangocms-cascade/blob/master/cmsplugin_cascade/segmentation/cms_plugins.py#L135-L136

I submitted a pull request with a fix here: https://github.com/jrief/djangocms-cascade/pull/206

The issue can be reproduced by editing a CMS page, switching to 'structure' view and adding: container->simple wrapper->segment

issue_title: "toc" option always appends
issue_body:
Currently, the table of contents is always appended to, which causes a problem if the Markdown object is persistent.  For example, the following script will produce HTML for many Markdown files:

```python
import markdown2
import os.path as path
import argparse

parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument('md_files', type=str, nargs='+', help='Markdown filename(s) to be parsed')
args = parser.parse_args()

markdowner = markdown2.Markdown(extras=["code-friendly", "fenced-code-blocks", "header-ids", "toc"])
html_header = """<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">

<head>
<meta charset="utf-8">
<link rel="stylesheet" type="text/css" href="./github.css">
</head>

<body>"""

for f in args.md_files:
    with open(f, 'r') as fh:
    	 html_file = open(path.splitext(path.basename(f))[0] + '.html', 'w')
         html_file.write(html_header)
	 html_file.write(markdown2.markdown("## Table of Contents"))
	 html_body = markdowner.convert(fh.read())
	 html_file.write(html_body.toc_html)
	 html_file.write(html_body)
	 html_file.write("</body\n</html>")
	 html_file.close()
```

If there is more than one file, each file will include the Table of Contents from the previous files with the current file's TOC appended to the end.  This is less than ideal behavior.  The only current alternative is to add in the line `markdowner._toc = None` when done with the current file, but this is supposed to be a "private" variable, so it would be better if there was some method of resetting the table of contents variable.

issue_title: Negative quantile approximation with high skew/less data
issue_body:
```
digest = TDigest()
digest.batch_update([62.0, 202.0, 1415.0, 1433.0])
digest.percentile(0.25)
```

Returns `-136.25`. This is because in https://github.com/CamDavidsonPilon/tdigest/blob/master/tdigest/tdigest.py#L166-L167, `delta` is computed as the mean of the means of the neighbouring centroids and is used as the slope to linearly approximate the quantile between the two centroids. In the following line, `m_i + ((p - t) / k - 1/2)*delta` is negative because `delta` is very large, and `p - t = 0` and thus the expression evaluates to `m_i + (-1/2)*delta` which is negative.

When using custom meta data i noticed you must use "revision" as the name of the field which is the FK to Revision. Anything else gives you an error. Took me a while to figure it out. Might be worth clarifying in the doco?

http://django-reversion.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api.html#custom-meta-data 

```python
from reversion.models import Revision

class VersionRating(models.Model):

```python
from reversion.models import Revision

class VersionRating(models.Model):

    rating = models.PositiveIntegerField()
```

issue_title: [feature request] Permissions for the admin
issue_body:
Hi,

I see this matter has already been suggested (#560  #287), but would like reopen the case. I think having a specific permission to restore versions would be a great improvement.

It has been argued that restoring a version is basically the same than editing an entry. That's technically right, but it's quite more complex from an user point of view, when there are inlines, complex widgets, or specific business logic behind the edits. 

Here at least, we have some users that are only quickly trained to enter some data, and some other users that are better trained for more complex administrative tasks. I have to choose between disabling VersionAdmin altogether, or hoping un-trained users won't click on the history button to mess around with old versions.

I think a restore_revision permission would fit perfectly in the app. I don't know the code base, but am pretty sure it would be very easy to implement. Of course I could implement it here, but if there's no way this is going to land in master, it's not very encouraging.

Thanks for the awesome work !

issue_title: Aggregation on annotated field fails if queryset ordered by other values + set default value
issue_body:
Hey Marts,

First of all thanks for the package, it simple but very useful :)

I have a couple of comments/questions:

**1. Aggregation on annotated field** 
I have noticed that you do not reorder the QuerySet before aggregation, which causes problems when aggregating on an annotated field, if it is already ordered by another field.

For example, consider the model:
```
class Blog(models.Model):
    date = models.DateField()
    category = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    content = models.TextField(default='')
    
    class Meta:
        order_by = ['date']
```
If I pivot on an annotated field like:
```
qs = Blog.objects.annotate(period=Trunc('date', 'year'))
pivot(qs, 'period', 'category', 'content', Count)
```
Aggregation does not work because the 'date' field must be part of the query's _group by_ for the ordering to work. 

Reordering fixes the problem, so this will work:
```
qs = Blog.objects.annotate(period=Trunc('date', 'year')).order_by('period')
pivot(qs, 'period', 'category', 'content', Count)
```
I think it would make sense to implement this in the package.

**2. Default value**
The default column value is `None` atm. Why not using 0? Alternatively could one set the default value at module level? Something like:
```
def _get_annotations(column, column_values, data, aggregation):
    value = data if hasattr(data, 'resolve_expression') else F(data)
    return {
        display_value: aggregation(Case(When(Q(**{column: column_value}), then=value), default=DEFAULT_VALUE))
        for column_value, display_value in column_values
    }
```

**3. Cumulative aggregation**
It would be fairly easy to allow for cumulative aggregation as well, which can be useful. It can be done using the window function (+distinct):
```
window = Window(aggregator(data), order_by=row)
```
It does not work on sqlite though...
I'd be happy to have a look at it if you think it could be useful.

issue_title: how to convert a numpy string array into the correct numpy array encodings for face recognition comparison ?
Support key rotation
issue_body: Unless it's not documented, there is no way to rotate AES keys in production while maintaining historic data integrity. We would probably need something like `manage.py rotate_aes_keys --key_name=... --old-key-file=...` and that could loop through and update all fields with the new key. This might cause intermittent errors while the update is in progress so perhaps there is a better way to do it.

issue_title: Handle Homgears listMethods-implementation?
issue_body: When calling ListMethod, Homegear supplies an additional paramter, which the official XML-API does not. Should we implement or ignore this? The CCU won't send that extra parameter, so supporting HGs implementation could break the CCU. And since CCU is the official, I'm in favour of the current approach.

issue_title: Enhancement Request, Display Int values in print statement, cast int values to GP process
issue_body:
Is it possible to have a better method print to int values? 
(Even better to be able to cast them to the GP as ints)

Simple example, with Random Forests:

```python
RFC(n_estimators=int(n_estimators),
        min_samples_split=int(min_samples_split),
        max_features=min(max_features, 0.999),
        random_state=2)
```

When running it may print out: 
`n_estimators = 10.3456, min_samples_split = 2.35643, max_features=0.99`

I would expect it to print out:
`n_estimators = 10, min_samples_split = 2, max_features=0.99`

Going deeper, because its floats that have been passed, it may search the 'same' space again:
```python
n_estimators = 10.3456, min_samples_split = 2.35643, max_features=0.99
n_estimators = 10.6334, min_samples_split = 2.12329, max_features=0.99
```
It seems rather wasteful to search `int` spaces.

I say this with no idea on how this affects the underlying GP process that is being called

[gwidion@localhost multi_key_dict]$ python3 multi_key_dict.py

issue_title: Locale Identifier With Territory Coerced Improperly
issue_body:
[This](https://github.com/kvesteri/sqlalchemy-utils/commit/dc83308c28e38f0be1bbfd46fa837f165f8aa73f) commit fixes parsing of Locale strings that include a territory in the identifier. However, the same issue still exists in `_coerce` on line 74. It should use `Locale.parse` as well.

If that is done, the `isinstance(value, Locale)` check on line 73 will be obsolete as `Locale.parse` already does this check. `_coerce` and `process_result_value` can be exactly the same.

issue_title: dateutil.zoneinfo.gettz is deprecated
issue_body:
See note in http://dateutil.readthedocs.io/en/stable/zoneinfo.html#dateutil.zoneinfo.gettz

It looks like changing the import of `gettz` from `dateutil.zoneinfo` to `dateutil.tz` in the `TimezoneType` init would fix it, unless the dateutil-specific zoneinfo is desired here.

issue_title: Relax restriction on callbacks from modal dialogs
issue_body:
Some people would prefer to use member functions for their modal dialog callbacks, but this is not safe to clone when handling screen resizing.

One option may be to allow programmers to decide whether they want to maintain the modal dialog over resizing (and so stick to this restriction) or cancel the dialog on resize (and so allowing this restriction to be lifted).  The choice could be enforced through a new keyword parameter on the constructor.

Some feedback on this idea would be appreciated.

issue_title: Putting .mo files into repository
issue_body: As I've understood, Flask-Admin is [considered](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/issues/626) to be an example of well-done internationalized Flask extension. They [put](https://github.com/flask-admin/flask-admin/tree/master/flask_admin/translations/de/LC_MESSAGES) compiled .mo files along with .po files into repository (and thus, PyPi distribution), because it is really [inconvenient](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/issues/622) to run `python setup.py compile_catalog` manually (e.g. how do I run it when installing Flask-Security from requirements.txt with CI?). Maybe, Flask-Security should also do so?

issue_title: string format containing {} not reported
issue_body:
matplotlib [1.4.x](https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/commit/21f46ff14ea65eeb725a4fd6c36642dddf3fea79)  [supposedly](http://matplotlib.org/users/whats_new.html) supports Python 2.6, however it has quite a few use of `{}`.

In [/doc/make.py](https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/21f46ff14ea65eeb725a4fd6c36642dddf3fea79/doc/make.py), line [264](https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/21f46ff14ea65eeb725a4fd6c36642dddf3fea79/doc/make.py#L264) is reported as an error, but this plugin doesnt catch the case a few lines nearby on line 261, 269, and 274.

Only a tiny thing, but the comment at https://github.com/bittner/django-apptemplates/blob/95b6fcdecf28e63e040f081b3f9cb782b9e18b15/tests.py#L7-L10 implies `django.test.utils` is the current location, but that is the old location – on https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/topics/testing/tools/#django.test.override_settings it says: "Changed in Django 1.7: Previously, override_settings was imported from django.test.utils."

I'm not sure why this got swapped round in https://github.com/bittner/django-apptemplates/commit/623842bafe15e0ca5692ffe03a45b6bf479f9b7e#diff-f4bc68ff01eef7bed2f3a64cad7d37ca but the other `try` import in https://github.com/bittner/django-apptemplates/blob/95b6fcdecf28e63e040f081b3f9cb782b9e18b15/apptemplates/__init__.py#L9-L12 puts the more recent location first, so I thought this should match. But as I say, only a tiny thing :)

issue_title: Authenticate with a field that is not set as the custom User model's USERNAME_FIELD
issue_body:
Hello @etianen,

I have a custom User model like this:

```python
class User(AbstractBaseUser, PermissionsMixin):
    identifier = models.UUIDField(default=uuid.uuid4, unique=True)
    email = models.EmailField(max_length=255, unique=True, null=True)

    USERNAME_FIELD = 'identifier'
```

I want to authenticate to ldap using the 'email' field instead of the 'identifier' field which is set in USERNAME_FIELD. I do not want to change USERNAME_FIELD because other authentication backends will use the 'identifier' field for authentication. By looking in the code, it seems that I have to manipulate  the resolve_user_identifier function to do this. So, do you think that an LDAP_AUTH_RESOLVE_USER_IDENTIFIER setting for passing a custom callable similar to LDAP_AUTH_CLEAN_USER_DATA is a good idea? Or is there another way?

issue_title: read_only function should also prevent field from populating the data
issue_body:
I am trying to create a form with some fields that should not be writable by the user. By using `read_only` function the html side works as expected, but it seems that the field still populates the data when `form.populate_obj` is called. I can't see any case where we would like to have html side say that the field is read_only but still save the changes that the user has made to that field. Therefore I would expect that calling `read_only` to a field also disables populating of that field during `form.populate_obj` function call.

My current workaround is to manually remove all read_only fields from the form before calling `form.populate_obj`.

issue_title: High failure-rate of Django-registration in slow mobile internet environment
issue_body:
@macropin 
I have a text-based app that lets users in low-resource localities communicate with one another over extremely flaky mobile internet (GPRS). It's being used in villages in South Asia, by people who can't afford great devices nor live in localities with robust coverage.  

My app utilizes django-registration to enable basic registration. I'm getting a complaint from ~50% of my users that registration fails in the app (we've extensively tested its robustness on our end). By failure they mean typing out the necessary fields and pressing register, only for the 'webpage not found' page to result. 

My app's inbuilt features (e.g. posting messages) are doing fine; registrations is the only one that is seeing an elevated complaint rate. Can there be any correlation between django-registration failing and slow mobile internet? Being a core contributer to this project, maybe you can send some pearls of wisdom my way. I'll be grateful. We really need a solution.
