issue_title: Flaky JavaScript tests omnibus ticket
issue_body: Our CasperJS tests have a reputation for being at times flaky, primarily due to race conditions and the asynchronous nature of javascript. I am opening this ticket so we can document any time we find Continuous Integration (Travis, etc) tests are failing, so we can track down the race conditions and eliminate them.

issue_title: Update the Roadmap
issue_body:
Our roadmap is a year old and no longer reflects the currently ongoing development paths. I'd like to form this roadmap together and for others to contribute to the roadmap over time as we evolve. Admittedly, much of the time I live out of issues and PRs and less in the markdown documents we create. That ends up making me personally less accessible (up in my head) and also makes it harder for people to take an at-a-glance look at the project.

We've accomplished quite a bit over the past few years:

* nteract desktop is now in beta stages
* We publish components and libraries from this repo for many others to use, including our own projects for sharing (commuter) and in-editor computational exploration (hydrogen)
* Hydrogen development has supercharged, resulting in over 50 releases since joining the organization. Hydrogen is even a [featured package](https://atom.io/packages)!
* We have a fantastic site
* We have the beginnings of a serverextension for jupyter
* Commuter uses our notebook-preview and transform components for viewing notebooks and other assets
* We have pushed several new protocols and mimetypes for the wider Jupyter ecosystem and seen them get adopted across all the frontends
* Encouraged collaboration and participation to scale our project out
* Contributed to a shared ecosystem within JavaScript and Python communities

There are some hidden technical threads we've focused on underneath the product portion:

* Shape interactions to target a wide breadth of users
* Keep state consistent, serializable, and able to move to the server side (precursors to realtime)

It's time to unify these into our overall roadmap and vision. I'm probably missing some likely things. In the interest of getting myself out of writer's block and general consternation, I'll post this issue and we can get things rolling.

issue_title: TM: Change three main functions into traditional methods
issue_body:
In the TemporalMemory's [top-level column loop](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/18ef587aba3654440dd8db20e87e2d96894e8b4c/src/nupic/research/temporal_memory.py#L167), we dispatch columns to one of three functions:

- activatePredictedColumn
- burstColumn
- punishPredictedColumn

These three functions exist for readability. We could paste their contents directly into the top-level loop, and that's originally how my code was written, but it made sense to "name" these procedures and move them out of the main loop.

These are actually static methods, which is essentially the same thing as a function.

There are two things here that I don't like:

- They take enormous argument lists. Almost every `self.*` attribute gets passed in. This adds potential for bugs, since you have to carefully get the parameters in the right order. And it makes the code harder to read. It's easier to understand code that you can fit on your screen all at once, and these long argument lists make it where you can't see the whole top-level column loop at once.
- It's a missed opportunity for extensibility. Even if we switch to overridable `classmethod`s, if the methods don't receive the `self` that severely limits the ability to add functionality. For example, the [FaultyTemporalMemory]() needs to access `self.deadCells`.

I'd like to convert these to traditional methods which receive a `self`, and stop passing in `self.*` parameters.

    return cls(encoding=encoding, **kw).decode(s)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/json/decoder.py", line 366, in decode
    obj, end = self.raw_decode(s, idx=_w(s, 0).end())
TypeError: expected string or buffer
```

This error is misleading because it looks like a JSON parsing error, but it's really because one of the swarm jobs failed and the swarming system is not extracting the error proplery from that job and displaying it to the user. The `jobInfo` object returned from the swarm job has no `results` object in this case, which is causing the error above. 

Instead, the program should report back the original error from the swarm job to the user instead of this worthless stacktrace.

issue_title: anomaly_likelihood_test: Has Broken Test (Fix is in comment)
issue_body:
## The text below contains both the description of the problem, and the fix to make the test work, and the code improvement of the class being tested.
 
**.../nupic/tests/unit/nupic/algorithms/anomaly_likelihood_test.py line#226**
testEstimateAnomalyLikelihoodsMalformedRecords() - ***actually tests nothing.***

#### the lines:

data1 = _generateSampleData(mean=0.2)
data1 = data1 + [(2, 2), (2, 2, 2, 2), (), (2)]  # Malformed records
    
likelihoods, avgRecordList, estimatorParams = (
      an.estimateAnomalyLikelihoods(data1[0:1000])
)

#### should be changed to:

data1 = _generateSampleData(mean=0.2)
data1 = data1**[0:996]** + [(2, 2), (2, 2, 2, 2), (), (2)]  # Malformed records
    
likelihoods, avgRecordList, estimatorParams = (
      an.estimateAnomalyLikelihoods(data1[0:1000])
)

... so that when estimateAnomalyLikelihoods() is called with **data1[0:1000]** , the "malformed" records will be included.

##### A corresponding change is needed in anomaly_likelihood.py in order to have the 2nd to last record not cause an exception (i.e. the "()" in  [(2, 2), (2, 2, 2, 2), (), (2)]). 

_This is so because the test method as previously written never actually passed the above malformed records in to the method being tested; now that they are being passed in, the code which "detects" bad records is now being executed._

**The required change is on line#486 in the _anomalyScoreMovingAverage() method:**

#### Instead of:
if len(record) != 3:
   if verbosity >= 0:
      print "Malformed record:", record
      continue

#### Should be:
if **not isinstance(record, list) or** len(record) != 3:
   if verbosity >= 0:
      print "Malformed record:", record
      continue

issue_title: Explore Improving Parameter Handling
issue_body:
Currently, we have a select set of parameters, some of which overrides any setting given by the default config.yaml (and possibly passed in user's config.yaml).  I think we should explore allowing for any parameter to be passed in that can override its corresponding value in a config.yaml file.

One approach as suggested by Loren: "We can convert config.yaml to config.py so we can do more logic directly there, and we can add a function to manipulate a user-provided config.yaml and override the config dict. Using this we can use function to import and iterate over config dict in cli.py to figure out what the params are that are permissible as arguments."

issue_title: Add generator support to MultiScene
issue_body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
Just an optimization.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
Right now the MultiScene must be given list/tuple of Scene objects. If we could provide an iterable then we could do some fancier operations. My use case is a days worth (or more) of full disk ABI files. It takes time to sort through all of those files. It takes a long time just to create the Scene objects and then give them to the MultiScene, not to mention each operation done on them.

It may be "nicest" for the user if we make this a keyword argument on the MultiScene like `use_generators=True` (False is the default). So doing things like resampling are actually just a generator that will do the resampling in the future.

**Describe any changes to existing user workflow**
Are there any backwards compatibility concerns?
Any users expecting to have access to length of the number of scenes in the multiscene or to index in to the number of scenes, would not be able to do it with a generator. The multiscene interface is technically experimental so I don't know if this needs to be a huge concern.

**Additional python or other dependencies**
None

**Describe any changes required to the build process**
None

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
...not using generators, I guess.

**Additional context**
Add any other context or screenshots about the feature request here.

issue_title: Possibly inefficient array segmenting
issue_body:
I was looking through some of the nearest neighbor and kdtree stuff and found this section of code:

https://github.com/pytroll/pyresample/blob/master/pyresample/kd_tree.py#L337

        if segments > 1:
        # Iterate through segments
        for i, target_slice in enumerate(geometry._get_slice(segments,
                                                             target_geo_def.shape)):

            # Query on slice of target coordinates
            next_voi, next_ia, next_da = \
                _query_resample_kdtree(resample_kdtree, source_geo_def,
                                       target_geo_def,
                                       radius_of_influence, target_slice,
                                       neighbours=neighbours,
                                       epsilon=epsilon,
                                       reduce_data=reduce_data,
                                       nprocs=nprocs)

            # Build result iteratively
            if i == 0:
                # First iteration
                valid_output_index = next_voi
                index_array = next_ia
                distance_array = next_da
            else:
                valid_output_index = np.append(valid_output_index, next_voi)
                if neighbours > 1:
                    index_array = np.row_stack((index_array, next_ia))
                    distance_array = np.row_stack((distance_array, next_da))
                else:
                    index_array = np.append(index_array, next_ia)
                    distance_array = np.append(distance_array, next_da)

This code is saving memory by processing the data in chunks, but the end result will be the same size. So it is probably faster to allocate the entire end result arrays and write to the appropriate chunk rather than appending (which reallocates and copies the previous array).

issue_title: double save turns process into zombie on OS X
issue_body:
A very easy way to turn my python process into a zombie that can't be killed even with -9, and blocks my Terminal tab:

```
from annoy import *
import random

def main():
    f = 10
    t = AnnoyIndex(f)
    for i in xrange(0):
        v = [random.gauss(0, 1) for z in xrange(f)]
        t.add_item(i, v)
    t.build(-1)
    t.save("t.ann")
    t.save("t.ann")
```

In fact, this is all I need:
```
from annoy import *
t = AnnoyIndex(10)
t.save("t.ann")
t.save("t.ann")
```

issue_title: QueryParameterAuthentication
issue_body:
Hello @jpadilla,

Recently I had to implement an authenticator that received the token via query parameters. Use case is creating a download file button that hits an endpoint e.g. /api/reports/somereport?export=csv

I thought I'd paste it here to see if you would like it added to the lib, maybe others could use it?

```
import jwt

from rest_framework_jwt.settings import api_settings
from rest_framework_jwt.authentication import JSONWebTokenAuthentication


class QueryParameterAuthentication(JSONWebTokenAuthentication):

    def authenticate(self, request):
        """
        Parses the request's Query Parameters for a `token` and sets it
        on the authorization header for authentication.
        """
        if 'token' not in request.QUERY_PARAMS:
            return None

        token = request.QUERY_PARAMS['token']
        header = '{} {}'.format(api_settings.JWT_AUTH_HEADER_PREFIX, token)
        request.META['HTTP_AUTHORIZATION'] = header

        return super(QueryParameterAuthentication, self).authenticate(request)
```

issue_title: Model for arrays
issue_body:
Hello,

This may look more like a feature request but I noticed that the models returned by Z3 changed greatly between 4.4.0 and 4.8.0. On the following file [model.smt2.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/2219616/model.smt2.txt), I get very different result for the model. For context of this request, I use a tool that automatically parse these models to generate useful output for the end-user.
For arrays, I would previously get:
```
unknown
(model 
[...]
  (define-fun m () (Array Int character)
    (_ as-array k!1))
  (define-fun k!1 ((x!0 Int)) character
    (ite (= x!0 0) character!val!0
      character!val!0))
)
```

Now, I get:
```
(define-fun m () (Array Int character)
    (lambda ((x!1 Int)) character!val!0))
```

It looks difficult to me to differentiate a function and an array. Would it be possible to add `as-array` in front of the `lambda` in this (or restore previous behavior?) ? 
```
(define-fun m () (Array Int character)
    (as-array (lambda ((x!1 Int)) character!val!0)))

```
Also, if I replace `c_vc_constant` with `lambda` ( [model2.smt2.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/2219704/model2.smt2.txt)) in the previous file, it is difficult to differenciate the keyword `lambda` of your output and the name of the constant.

Finally, do you have any discussions (internally) or with other tools developers to agree on a standard for model (I am thinking of something similar to smt-lib) ?

issue_title: Java Bindings generate two different SMT2 models on Linux or MacOS
issue_body:
When using quantifiers in our program  (Same as issue #1112), executing z3 will generate a different SMT2 model in MacOS than on Linux.

This has been verified both on the oficial 4.6 release and on commit 1323b8f63f21f40b7a264140ff84afa3dad989c8.

An example is available in the following repo, along with instructions to run:
https://github.com/alcides/z3test 

The output of running our software in both OSes are available inside the `models_generated` folder. 
Since our software makes several checks, the first one that is different is highlighted in linux.txt and macos.txt (and ordered manually, to temporarily fix #1488).

Our program is a bit complex, but has no OS-dependent code, besides the z3 so/dylibs. The only place it might be different is if z3 does things differently for OSes (I know that some features are unsupported, but this model is supported on both OSes).

issue_title: (X-)Forwarded not compatible with AF_INET6
issue_body:
Hello,

The X-Forwarded and Forwarded middleware is not compatible with sockets created by AF_INET6. As per the [sockets](https://docs.python.org/3/library/socket.html#socket-families) documentation, for AF_INET6 a 4-tuple is used. The current [middleware](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp-remotes/blob/master/aiohttp_remotes/x_forwarded.py#L94) only handles 2-tuple case when sockets are created by AF_INET.

Since we only require the peer_ip, we would just take the first item of the tuple returned.

issue_title: Need clarification of purpose for `pure_c` option
issue_body:
The description of this option is here: 

> pure_c: (Default True) If your project is C++, pass the pure_c=False, it will add new build combinations using libstdc and libstdc++11 in the `setting compiler.libcxx`.

By default, `setting compiler.libcxx` is not used for hashing/ABI compatibility, so binaries built with `setting compiler.libcxx=libstdc` and `setting compiler.libcxx=libstdc++11` are identical, correct? 

I believe the primary purpose of conan-package-tools is to build the wide range of *different* binaries, so that users don't have to re-build themselves.  Since setting `pure_c=False` effectively doubles the number of builds on Linux and OSX, but half of the binaries are identical by default, then it would seem to be a bit redundant/wasteful to use this option. 

I can see one possible value proposition for this however, which is testing.  If someone wants to build their packages against both `stdc++` libraries under various compilers to make sure it works, then this makes sense.  Is this the primary purpose of exposing this option in conan-package-tools, or have I got something else wrong?

issue_title: Fix build failure problem
issue_body:
There is an intermittent build failure issue caused by some interaction with `updatezinfo.py` and the IANA time zone servers. It's not clear (to me) why we get so many failures, but it may have something to do with some rate-limiting that they haven't told us about.

I think the initial fix to this is to add a "wait-retry" loop to the Travis and Appveyor CI configurations. Longer term, I'm thinking we may want to separate out the timezone metadata and rehost the tzfiles at that repo, and the IANA server can be considered a mirror.

issue_title: tzname() inconsistent between tzwin() and tzwinlocal() and neither reflect actual DST.
issue_body:
Time zone names are inconsistent between `tzwin()` and `tzwinlocal()` and neither reflect actual DST.

Tested on Windows for all US time zones with current master SHA1: b46e721 and for proposed fix at #142 - both fail.

```python
import datetime
import dateutil.tz

tw = dateutil.tz.tzwin('Eastern Standard Time')
twl = dateutil.tz.tzwinlocal()
tests = [('EDT should be started', '2015-03-08 03:01'),
         ('Still not EDT', '2015-03-15 01:59'),
         ('tzwin is EDT', '2015-03-15 02:00'),
         ('tzwinlocal still not EDT', '2015-03-16 23:59'),
         ('Finally EDT!', '2015-03-17 00:00'),
         ('EDT should have ended', '2015-11-01 03:01'),
         ('Still not EST', '2015-11-08 01:59'),
         ('tzwin is EST', '2015-11-08 02:00'),
         ('tzwinlocal still not EST', '2015-11-15 23:59'),
         ('Finally EST!', '2015-11-16 00:00')]

row = '{:<16}  |  {:<21}  |  {:<21}  |  {}'
print row.format('Date', 'tzwin', 'tzwinlocal', 'Notes')
print '{:-<100}'.format('')
for each in tests:
    desc, date = each
    test = datetime.datetime.strptime(date, '%Y-%m-%d %H:%M')
    tw_test = test.replace(tzinfo=tw)
    twl_test = test.replace(tzinfo=twl)
    print row.format(date, tw_test.tzname(), twl_test.tzname(), desc)
```
result:
```
Date              |  tzwin                  |  tzwinlocal             |  Notes
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

issue_title: JournalDB makes ChainDB.persist_trie_data_dict() up to 20x slower on a fully populated DB
issue_body:
`ChainDB.persist_trie_data_dict()` is yet another bottleneck I've found when running a full-node sync: it takes between 3 to 9 seconds to persist a batch of (128) bodies or (256) receipts, and most of that was spent on `JournalDB.set()`, which does a `wrapped_db.get()` before setting the new value.

This is specially problematic when performing a chain sync, but at the same a chain sync doesn't need a journaled DB, so maybe in that case we can just use a custom, non-journaled, DB class? Can anybody think of possible problems with that? Also, does that mean we'd need to extend trinity's DB-serving process to also offer a non-journaled chaindb?

issue_title: Executing arbitrary modules or entry points
issue_body:
Are there any plans to support executing modules directly (like `python -m http.server`) or running different entry points from the same file (like `PEX_SCRIPT=gunicorn my.pex`)? (Or would there be any opposition for supporting this functionality?)

We have a monolithic codebase that has a single set of Python requirements, but different ways of running things. For our web apps, we run `gunicorn`, but for some other services, we directly call `python` on a file. It would be great to be able to build once, and then be able to support both runtime situations, like one can with PEX.

For now, I have a workaround that just creates a dead simple Python file that instantiates and runs a gunicorn `WSGIApplication` object directly, but that is not ideal.

issue_title: Read does not honor the timeout on Linux
issue_body:
Hello,
  Reading from the serial port with a timeout is not reliable on my system:
* Linux Mint Nadia
* Python 2.7.3
* pyserial 2.7 (but also 2.5) installed both with ```pip``` and the latest tar.gz

I have a CDC-ACM dongle that transmits data periodically.

This simple program does not respect the timeout:

```
import serial
import time

ser = serial.Serial(
                    port        = '/dev/ttyACM0',
                    baudrate    = 460800,
                    bytesize    = serial.EIGHTBITS,
                    parity      = serial.PARITY_NONE,
                    stopbits    = serial.STOPBITS_ONE,
                    timeout     = 0.1
                   )

while True:
   length = 1000000

   t0 = time.time()
   print str(len(ser.read(length))) + ' ' + str((time.time() - t0))
```

Running the above I get:

```
123 0.109111070633
246 0.205754995346
123 0.106971025467
369 0.310961008072
123 0.106863975525
369 0.309740066528
246 0.214066982269
```
Data is read but with an higher latency.
Things go worst if the timeout is set to 1s, in which case data is read with higher latencies (>> 1s).

What's going on? For comparison I've tried the following program that mimics what ```pyserial``` should do:

```
import serial
import time
import select

ser = serial.Serial(
                    port        = '/dev/ttyACM0',
                    baudrate    = 460800,
                    bytesize    = serial.EIGHTBITS,
                    parity      = serial.PARITY_NONE,
                    stopbits    = serial.STOPBITS_ONE,
                    timeout     = None, #block forever
                   )

def read(ser, length, timeout):
   n = 0
   b = bytearray()
   ser.timeout = 0 #non-blocking
   while timeout >= 0 and n < length:
       t0 = time.time()
       ready = select.select([ser], [], [], timeout)
       timeout -= (time.time() - t0)
       if len(ready[0]) != 0:
           bb = ser.read(length - n)
           n += len(bb)
           b.extend(bb)
   return b

while True:
   timeout = 0.1 #seconds
   length = 1000000

   t0 = time.time()
   b = read(ser, length, timeout)
   print str(len(b)) + ' ' + str((time.time() - t0))
```

Whose output is:

```

123 0.101146936417
123 0.101223945618
123 0.100612163544
123 0.100650072098
123 0.101171016693
123 0.10132598877
```

As you can see data is consistently read with the specified latency.

issue_title: suspicious statement "block[1] - block[0]" in partial_ratio
issue_body:
Hi,

I noticed [this line](https://github.com/seatgeek/fuzzywuzzy/blob/master/fuzzywuzzy/fuzz.py#L57) in the function `partial_ratio` is very suspicious.

```python
long_start = block[1] - block[0] if (block[1] - block[0]) > 0 else 0
```

The matching blocks returned from `SequenceMatcher` is of the form `(a,b,size)` where `a` (`block[0]`) and `b` (`block[1]`) are the indexes in *each* of the input strings, i.e. `s1[a:a+size]` should match `s2[b:b+size]`. It makes *no sense* to me to compute `b-a`, and use it as starting index of the long string.

I noticed there are several open and closed issues about the behavior of `partial_ratio`, #193 #191, #141 #99 to name a few. This may be the reason.

We can only merge items from contributors who signed the CLA

If you can identify potential problems, add suggestions, or even leave a comment with your review of another PR (Looks good to me! Or I approve this change), that would help us review those PRs much faster, making it easier to get to your PR.

**Do you even write tests, friend?** 
If you see a place where we could have a test to validate a piece of functionality, add it in. We know We know! It’s just another PR we have to review and merge. You’re right, it is! issue_title: `eth_getCompilers` is removed from recent ethereum
issue_body:
https://github.com/ConsenSys/mythril/blob/8d8f1bf6cd5dac9c5c3c311a214950c68f86224e/mythril/rpc/base_client.py#L428-L434

```
Exception: {'jsonrpc': '2.0', 'id': 1, 'error': {'code': -32601, 'message': 'The method eth_getCompilers does not exist/is not available'}}
```
`eth_getCompilers` is not available for neither of `ipc` nor `rpc`, and also the `compileXxx` methods are gone

You can see more here: <https://github.com/ethereum/go-ethereum/issues/3793>.

I will create a PR for this :)

issue_title: The documentation for blueprints isn't clear on how they work
issue_body:
I'd like to know how blueprints actually work in Flask-JSONRPC; the only thing the examples look to be doing is registering the blueprint itself, but the methods on it are registered to the toplevel JSONRPC instance, instead of one for the blueprint itself:

```python
from base_app import jsonrpc  # the one from the toplevel app
from flask import Blueprint

bp = Blueprint(__name__)

# if I had a method here:
@bp.route("/')
def method():
    pass

# but for the JSONRPC, the blueprint doesn't seem to be used:
@jsonrpc.method("BluePrint.a_method")
def a_method():
    pass
```

I'd expect something like this:

```python
from flask_jsonrpc import JSONRPC
from flask import Blueprint

bp = Blueprint(__name__)
bprpc = JSONRPC(blueprint=bp)

@bprpc.method("a_method")
def a_method():
    pass

from base_app import jsonrpc
jsonrpc.register_blueprint("TopLevel", bprpc)
```

This would end up being called as TopLevel.a_method.

I'm not sure this makes sense, but it lines up with my intuition about how blueprints work.

issue_title: Making cloudpickle produce "consistent/deterministic" results.
issue_body:
This question arose in the following context. I have multiple Python processes, and some classes are defined in each process. Sometimes class definitions are shipped from one process to another (using cloudpickle). Sometimes classes may be shipped multiple times or in multiple ways to a given process, and I'd like to deduplicate them based on the output of cloudpickle (that is, if `cloudpickle.dumps(class1) == cloudpickle.dumps(class2)`, then the classes are the "same" and I can throw away one of them. This works, but there are way too many false negatives (that is, two classes really are the same (in some sense), but `cloudpickle.dumps` gives different results on the two classes.

Here's one example that sort of illustrates the issue (although there are a number of ways this kind of thing can arise).

Suppose I do the following.

```python
import cloudpickle

class Foo1(object):
    def __init__(self):
        pass

serialized1 = cloudpickle.dumps(Foo1)
Foo2 = cloudpickle.loads(serialized1)
serialized2 = cloudpickle.dumps(Foo2)

assert serialized1 == serialized2  # This assertion fails.
```

I'd love for this kind of assertion to succeed. **Does anyone know if this is achievable or what the main obstacles are?**

Interestingly, if I iterate this a third time,

```python
Foo3 = cloudpickle.loads(serialized2)
serialized3 = cloudpickle.dumps(Foo3)

assert serialized2 == serialized3  # This succeeds.
```

then the assert succeeds, so maybe it suffices to use `cloudpickle.dumps(cloudpickle.loads(cloudpickle.dumps(cls)))` to deduplicate classes (though this seems kind of insane, and I haven't tested this extensively). **Would you expect this to work?**

One thing that may be related/revealing is the outputs I get if I do something similar in an IPython interpreter (instead of a regular Python interpreter). 

First copy and paste this block into IPython.

```python
import cloudpickle

class Foo(object):
    def __init__(self):
        pass

serialized1 = cloudpickle.dumps(Foo)
```

Then copy and paste this block into IPython.

```python
class Foo(object):
    def __init__(self):
        pass

serialized2 = cloudpickle.dumps(Foo)
```

Comparing `serialized1` and `serialized2` next to each other, they are

```
serialized1  # b'\x80\x02ccloudpickle.cloudpickle\n_rehydrate_skeleton_class\nq\x00(ccloudpickle.cloudpickle\n_builtin_type\nq\x01X\t\x00\x00\x00ClassTypeq\x02\x85q\x03Rq\x04X\x03\x00\x00\x00Fooq\x05c__builtin__\nobject\nq\x06\x85q\x07}q\x08X\x07\x00\x00\x00__doc__q\tNs\x87q\nRq\x0b}q\x0c(X\n\x00\x00\x00__module__q\rX\x08\x00\x00\x00__main__q\x0eX\x08\x00\x00\x00__init__q\x0fccloudpickle.cloudpickle\n_fill_function\nq\x10(ccloudpickle.cloudpickle\n_make_skel_func\nq\x11h\x01X\x08\x00\x00\x00CodeTypeq\x12\x85q\x13Rq\x14(K\x01K\x00K\x01K\x01KCc_codecs\nencode\nq\x15X\x04\x00\x00\x00d\x00S\x00q\x16X\x06\x00\x00\x00latin1q\x17\x86q\x18Rq\x19N\x85q\x1a)X\x04\x00\x00\x00selfq\x1b\x85q\x1cX\x1e\x00\x00\x00<ipython-input-1-d9b5c81388ae>q\x1dh\x0fK\x04h\x15X\x02\x00\x00\x00\x00\x01q\x1eh\x17\x86q\x1fRq ))tq!Rq"J\xff\xff\xff\xff}q#\x87q$Rq%}q&N}q\'NtRutR.'
serialized2  # b'\x80\x02ccloudpickle.cloudpickle\n_rehydrate_skeleton_class\nq\x00(ccloudpickle.cloudpickle\n_builtin_type\nq\x01X\t\x00\x00\x00ClassTypeq\x02\x85q\x03Rq\x04X\x03\x00\x00\x00Fooq\x05c__builtin__\nobject\nq\x06\x85q\x07}q\x08X\x07\x00\x00\x00__doc__q\tNs\x87q\nRq\x0b}q\x0c(X\n\x00\x00\x00__module__q\rX\x08\x00\x00\x00__main__q\x0eX\x08\x00\x00\x00__init__q\x0fccloudpickle.cloudpickle\n_fill_function\nq\x10(ccloudpickle.cloudpickle\n_make_skel_func\nq\x11h\x01X\x08\x00\x00\x00CodeTypeq\x12\x85q\x13Rq\x14(K\x01K\x00K\x01K\x01KCc_codecs\nencode\nq\x15X\x04\x00\x00\x00d\x00S\x00q\x16X\x06\x00\x00\x00latin1q\x17\x86q\x18Rq\x19N\x85q\x1a)X\x04\x00\x00\x00selfq\x1b\x85q\x1cX\x1e\x00\x00\x00<ipython-input-2-a08e1f07615d>q\x1dh\x0fK\x02h\x15X\x02\x00\x00\x00\x00\x01q\x1eh\x17\x86q\x1fRq ))tq!Rq"J\xff\xff\xff\xff}q#\x87q$Rq%}q&N}q\'NtRutR.'

issue_title: Calling model_admin.get_queryset with test Request breaks plugins
issue_body:
In utils.py, line 183, you create a fake request object, and pass this to model_admin.get_queryset() on line 186.

However, this completely overrides changes made to the request object in Django middleware. In particular, some plugins, Django-Guardian in particular, is expecting to see a request object which has passed through the site middleware.

In the case of Django-Guardian, a row-level permissions app, it expects to find the current User object on Request. Because you are passing it a fake Request object, the "user" attribute does not exist.

I fixed the problem by changing utils.get_model_queryset, adding an additional parameter: context, and retrieving the actual Request object from the context.

I then pass the context to utils.get_model_queryset in templatetages/jet_tags.jet_sibling_object_url()

However, there still seem to be issues with how the Guardian templates are interacting with the Jet templates that I have yet to resolve.

issue_title: Proposal: Log SystemExit in wsgi wrapper
issue_body:
When using gunicorn, at least, and a worker times out, gunicorn sends a `SIGABRT` signal to the worker process. This manifests itself as a `SystemExit` exception that gets raised in the worker.

When this happens in Django, Django completely ignores `SystemExit` and lets it re-raise so the process can exit, without ever calling the `got_request_exception` signal, so in our case, we never see the exception and nobody knows that anything happened since it happily exited without a fuss.

My proposal would be to explicitly catch `SystemExit`, log, then reraise.

imo this seems like a reasonable thing to do within a middleware. If something is trying to shut down the process while in the middle of the request, probably worth logging.

In gunicorn, this wouldn't log for a normal shutdown, since that sends a `SIGTERM` instead of `SIGABRT`.

Would need to be tested against other wsgi runners, specifically uwsgi.

Refs:
https://github.com/django/django/blob/37ea3cb03e80de80380009a7a7939bc48d75abe9/django/core/handlers/base.py#L223-L225
https://github.com/benoitc/gunicorn/blob/e1912db59a2f1df36387dd28bb3515202cc03098/gunicorn/arbiter.py#L443

issue_title: Handle `fingerprint` argument on logging handler
middleware ClosingIterator() does not always call iterable.close()
issue_body:
I work at New Relic, and we have several customers who have reported the issue described in this post on the Sentry forum:

* https://forum.sentry.io/t/sentry-wsgi-wrapper-and-new-relic-apm/291

The issue is that customers who use both the Sentry WSGI middleware and the New Relic Python agent were seeing transaction times that were dramatically wrong. Durations were too long by orders of magnitude. When digging in to see why this happens, we narrowed the cause down to a recent change to how the Sentry WSGI middleware was calling `close()` on the iterable returned by the WSGI application. (See commit 10d0774a8c7d2e04e92dd97af1de7079a6441bb1 from Aug 19 as part of PR #836.)

And, it turns out that this impacts not just applications that use New Relic, but also any Django application.

## The problem

In `ClosingIterator.__init__()`, `self.iterable` is assigned the value of `iter(iterable)`:

```python
class ClosingIterator(Iterator):
    ...
    def __init__(self, sentry, iterable, environ):
        ...
        self.iterable = iter(iterable)
```

Later, in `ClosingIterator.close()`, there's a check to see if `self.iterable` has a `close()` method, and if it does, it calls it:

```python
class ClosingIterator(Iterator):
    ...
    def close(self):
        ...
        try:
            if hasattr(self.iterable, 'close'):
                with common_exception_handling(self.environ, self.sentry):
                    self.iterable.close()
```

The problem is that the original `iterable` and `self.iterable` are not necessarily the same object, which means that calling `self.iterable.close()` does not necessarily call `iterable.close()`. That's because calling `iter(iterable)` does not always return the original iterable, but instead it can return an entirely different iterator.

It all depends on how the original `iterable` is defined. If it is defined with an `__iter__` method that returns `self`, then everything is good. That means that `assert iterable is iter(iterable)` is `True`.

That's how the iterable from a Flask/Werkzeug application is defined. It's a `werkzeug.wsgi:ClosingIterator` object, where `__iter__` is defined like this:

```python
class ClosingIterator(object):
    ...
    def __iter__(self):
        return self
```

Example:

    In [61]: werk_resp = werkzeug.Response()

    In [62]: app_iter = werk_resp.get_app_iter({'REQUEST_METHOD': 'GET'})

    In [63]: app_iter
    Out[63]: <werkzeug.wsgi.ClosingIterator at 0x1039f9d10>

    In [64]: iter(app_iter)
    Out[64]: <werkzeug.wsgi.ClosingIterator at 0x1039f9d10>

However, there are other cases where `assert iterable is iter(iterable)` is `False` because what is returned from `iter(iterable)` is different from the original iterable. In Django, for example, `django.http.response:HttpResponse` defines `__iter__` like this:

```python
class HttpResponse(HttpResponseBase):
    ...
    def __iter__(self):
        return iter(self._container)
```

Example:

    In [52]: dj_resp = django.http.HttpResponse()

    In [53]: dj_resp
    Out[53]: <django.http.response.HttpResponse at 0x1039f9b90>

    In [54]: iter(dj_resp)
    Out[54]: <listiterator at 0x1039f9750>

When that's the case, calling `close()` on the result of `iter(iterable)` does not call the `close()` method on the original `iterable`.

## Possible solution

I think the answer is to save away the close method of the original `iterable`:

```python
class ClosingIterator(Iterator):
    ...
    def __init__(self, sentry, iterable, environ):
        ...
        self.iterable = iter(iterable)
        self.iterable_close = getattr(iterable, 'close', None)
```

so that it's possible to call it in `close()`:

```python
    def close(self):
        ...
        try:
            if self.iterable_close:
                with common_exception_handling(self.environ, self.sentry):
                    self.iterable_close()
```

I would be happy to submit a pull request with this fix (and tests), but I thought I would run this by you first before I did.

cc: @tkrajcar

issue_title: Conversion process should not rely on InstanceNumber
issue_body:
Problem 1: `InstanceNumber` is Type 2 attribute - it can be missing. As of current version of dicom2nifti, missing `InstanceNumber` results in no output produced.

Problem 2: There is also no hard rule that requires InstanceNumber to establish the spatial ordering of the frames. The only reliable way to sort instances and reconstruct volume is to sort them using `Image Position (Patient)` and `Image Orientation (Patient)` (also see related discussion in http://dclunie.blogspot.com/2013/10/how-thick-am-i-sad-story-of-lonely-slice.html), and this approach is currently not implemented in the tool.

Speaking for myself, until this issue is resolved, it is hard to seriously consider using dicom2nifti.

issue_title: Spec for revamped validation
issue_body:
#### Issue Checklist:
- [x] Descriptive title
- [x] Completed sections below

# Problem Description

We've dismissed Yamale as the tool to validate YAML format. We need line information and customised error messages. This document is the spec for the new validation system.

# Solution Proposal

##  Error Message Format

Each error message will print the line number and an excerpt of the content:

Template:
```
<FILE_PATH>:<LINE_NUMBER> -> <BLOCK_PATH>
<ERROR_MESSAGE>
```

Examples: 
```
./stream.yml:8: -> /Time
invalid python expression

./stream.yml:34: -> /Aggregates/Fields[3]
missing required property "Type"
```

## Errors

**Missing property**

```
./stream.yml:34: -> /Aggregates/Fields[3]
missing required property "Type"
```

**Missing block**

```
./stream.yml:0: -> /
missing required block "Aggregates"
```

**Invalid property**

```
./stream.yml:34: -> /Aggregates/Fields[3]/Tyyype
"Tyyype" is not a valid property 
```

**Invalid block**

```
./stream.yml:28: -> /Aggggregates
"Aggggregates" is not a valid block
```

**Invalid Data Type**

```
./stream.yml:36: -> /Aggregates/Fields[3]/Type
"blah" is not a valid data type 
```

**Invalid Identifier**

```
./stream.yml:35: -> /Aggregates/Fields[3]/Name
"n@me" is not a valid identifier
```
**Reserver Keyword**

```
./stream.yml:35: -> /Aggregates/Fields[3]/Name
"n@me" is not a valid identifier
```

**Invalid Python Expression**

Apart from the message, we'll print the output from Python syntax check:

```
./stream.yml:38: -> /Aggregates/Fields[3]/Value
invalid python expression:
   | <PYTHON_OUTPUT
   | <PYTHON_OUTPUT
```

# Impact on current behavior

The refactored validation will be disabled by default until implementation is complete. In the meantime, Yamale validation will be used, and there will be an available flag to use the new implementation.

issue_title: Refactor code to match extend_schema_spec with the patteren set by validate_schema_spec
issue_body:
#### Issue Checklist:
- [x] Descriptive title
- [x] Completed sections below

### Problem Description
The current function design for `extend_schema_spec` expects the spec to be passed to it for it to modify and return, even though the spec is already available in the current object.

### Solution Proposal
`extend_schema_spec` should follow the same pattern as `validate_schema_spec` which works on the data in the current object.  This reduces the amount of redundant variable passing and returning.

While at it, rename `_spec` in schema to `spec` without the `_` because there is no need to hide the spec in the schema.


### Impact on current behavior
None

import os
import pandas as pd
from rqalpha.interface import AbstractMod


__config__ = {
    "csv_path": None
}


def load_mod():
    return ExtendAPIDemoMod()


class ExtendAPIDemoMod(AbstractMod):
    def __init__(self):
        # 注入API 一定要在初始化阶段，否则无法成功注入
            self._csv_path = None
            self._inject_api()
    def start_up(self, env, mod_config):
            self._csv_path = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), mod_config.csv_path))

    def tear_down(self, code, exception=None):

    def _inject_api(self):
        from rqalpha import export_as_api
        from rqalpha.execution_context import ExecutionContext
        from rqalpha.const import EXECUTION_PHASE

        @export_as_api

issue_title: problem with transactions using f5-sdk 1.3.1 and 11.6.1
issue_body:
Testing on 11.6.1 w/ f5-sdk 1.3.1 ran into two issues with transactions

1. transaction ID is seen as an "int" and causes the request to fail
2. retrieving a "created" object messes up 'uri' and subsequent requests

I've patched my code to workaround the issues, not sure the best way to handle for the F5 Python SDK

Errors seen:

1. error creating dora.local.pcfdev.io Header value 1471619352790463 must be of type str or bytes, not <type 'int'>
2. 

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "cf2bigip-bad.py", line 592, in <module>
    sp.create_app(app_name, row[2], args.policy_name)
  File "cf2bigip-bad.py", line 339, in create_app
    pool_member = pool.members_s.members.create(partition=partition, name=member, priorityGroup=priority_group)
  File "/home/erchen/projects/pcf2bigip/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/f5/bigip/resource.py", line 851, in create
    return self._create(**kwargs)
  File "/home/erchen/projects/pcf2bigip/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/f5/bigip/resource.py", line 818, in _create
    response = session.post(_create_uri, json=kwargs, **requests_params)
  File "/home/erchen/projects/pcf2bigip/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/icontrol/session.py", line 230, in wrapper
    raise iControlUnexpectedHTTPError(error_message, response=response)
icontrol.exceptions.iControlUnexpectedHTTPError: 400 Unexpected Error: Bad Request for uri: https://10.1.10.240:443/mgmt/tm/transaction/1471619427763099/commands/2/members/
Text: u'{"code":400,"message":"Found invalid command id 2/members/","errorStack":[]}'
```

Code that avoids errors:

```
        with TransactionContextManager(self.tx) as api:
            # deal with ERROR #1: int vs. str
            api._meta_data['icr_session'].session.headers['X-F5-REST-Coordination-Id'] = api._meta_data['icr_session'].session.headers['X-F5-REST-Coordination-Id'].__str__()
            monitor = api.tm.ltm.monitor.https.http.create(name=monitor_name, partition=partition, interval=10, timeout=31, send=send_str, recv=recv_str)
            pool_path = "/%s/%s" % (partition, pool_name)

            pool = api.tm.ltm.pools.pool.create(partition=partition, name=pool_name, minActiveMembers=1, monitor=monitor_name)
            print "Created pool %s" % pool_path

            member_list = pool_members.split(',')
            member_list.reverse()
            priority_group = 0
            for member in member_list:
                # deal with ERROR #2 incorrect URI path
                pool._meta_data['uri'] = pool._meta_data['uri'].split("/transaction/")[0] + "/ltm/pool/~%s~%s/" %(partition,pool_name)
                pool_member = pool.members_s.members.create(partition=partition, name=member, priorityGroup=priority_group)
                priority_group += 10
                print " Added member %s" % member

            policy = api.tm.ltm.policys.policy.load(name = policy_name, partition = partition)

            rules = policy.rules_s.get_collection()

            my_rule = policy.rules_s.rules.create(name=app_name)

            payload = {u'caseInsensitive': True,
                   u'equals': True,
                   u'external': True,
                   u'fullPath': u'0',
                   u'host': True,
                   u'httpHost': True,
                   u'index': 0,
                   u'name': u'0',
                   u'present': True,
                   u'remote': True,
                   u'request': True,

issue_title: factory.Faker will always return the same value for successive calls
issue_body:
There is a major flaw in #275 & #354 . Faker will always return the same value for successive calls.

```
def test_expect_different_results(self):
        faker_field = factory.Faker('name')
        r1 = faker_field.generate({})
        r2 = faker_field.generate({})
        self.assertNotEqual(r1, r2)
```

This will fail. From what I see there are some options: 

1. use the random generator instance from faker
```
diff --git factory/random.py factory/random.py
index 88a5d9e..c8d73b7 100644
--- factory/random.py
+++ factory/random.py
@@ -1,8 +1,7 @@
 from __future__ import absolute_import
 
 import random
 
-randgen = random.Random()
+from faker.generator import random as randgen
```
2. synchronize the random generator from faker to factory boy after each call
```
diff --git factory/faker.py factory/faker.py
index 064863d..8bb08a7 100644
--- factory/faker.py
+++ factory/faker.py
@@ -22,7 +22,7 @@ import contextlib
 import faker
 import faker.config
 
-from .random import get_random_state
+from .random import get_random_state, set_random_state
 from . import declarations
 
 
@@ -50,7 +50,9 @@ class Faker(declarations.BaseDeclaration):
         kwargs.update(self.provider_kwargs)
         kwargs.update(extra_kwargs)
         subfaker = self._get_faker(self.locale)
-        return subfaker.format(self.provider, **kwargs)
+        generated_value = subfaker.format(self.provider, **kwargs)
+        set_random_state(subfaker.random.getstate())
+        return generated_value
 
     def evaluate(self, instance, step, extra):
         return self.generate(extra or {})
```
3. Add a hook after setting the state to synchronize additional random source
```
diff --git factory/faker.py factory/faker.py
index 064863d..87a04dd 100644
--- factory/faker.py
+++ factory/faker.py
@@ -22,7 +22,7 @@ import contextlib
 import faker
 import faker.config
 
-from .random import get_random_state
+from .random import get_random_state, set_random_state
 from . import declarations
 
 
@@ -76,8 +76,9 @@ class Faker(declarations.BaseDeclaration):
         if locale not in cls._FAKER_REGISTRY:
             subfaker = faker.Faker(locale=locale)
             cls._FAKER_REGISTRY[locale] = subfaker
+            subfaker.random.setstate(get_random_state())
+            set_random_state.hooks.append(subfaker.random.setstate)
 
-        cls._FAKER_REGISTRY[locale].random.setstate(get_random_state())
         return cls._FAKER_REGISTRY[locale]
 
     @classmethod
diff --git factory/random.py factory/random.py
index 88a5d9e..e535ba1 100644
--- factory/random.py
+++ factory/random.py
@@ -1,8 +1,7 @@
 from __future__ import absolute_import
 
 import random
-
-randgen = random.Random()
+from faker.generator import random as randgen
 
 randgen.state_set = False
 
@@ -15,9 +14,14 @@ def get_random_state():
 def set_random_state(state):
     """Force-set the state of factory.fuzzy's random generator."""
     randgen.state_set = True
+    for hook in set_random_state.hooks:
+        hook(state)
     return randgen.setstate(state)
 
 
+set_random_state.hooks = []
+
+
 def reseed_random(seed):
     """Reseed factory.fuzzy's random generator."""
     r = random.Random(seed)
```

The most elegant solution for me is the third option.

issue_title: Drillhole Database Reader
issue_body:
**Overview**
Drillhole functionality (and interpreting the results into 3D shapes) would fill a giant gap in open source geo-visualization. To address this, we will create a file reader that will handle the generation of VTK data objects from various types of drillhole databases. Basics would be:

- XYZ for the COLLAR (with some labeling attributes)
- SURVEY metadata for the azimuth and dip of the drill hole to plot its path
- incremental TABLES of information (e.g. depth from collar to describe geology, etc.)

**Implementation**
I'm currently thinking that we will have a data array selection window (checkboxes) for what drillholes to read then generate a multiblock dataset of the drillholes. As for whether each drillhole will be its own data object or groups of them will be distinguished will be determined as I dive deeper into the structures of these databases and what might be most useful.

**Additional Context**
- [Here](http://opengeostat.com/drillhole-database-design-2/ ) is an article on drilling databases with downloadable DDBs.
- [Geoblock](http://geoblock.sourceforge.net/) is an OS program that might have some available code.
- [Geoscience Analyst]( http://www.mirageoscience.com/our-products/software-product/geoscience-analyst) might also be worth checking out.
- Professional databases have a lot of fields in them describing the various technical aspects of drilling, but the common fields for all of them are `COLLAR`, `SURVEY`, `ASSAY`, and `LITHOLOGY`.

issue_title: recent changes seem to require adding Cython as a build dependency
issue_body:
Per the discussion in PyWavelets/pywt#321, there was a preference for distributing the generated C files in the PyPI sdist so that Cython is not a build-time dependency.

However, as part of the changes enabling C99 complex support in PyWavelets/pywt#300, some statements such as the following were added to the Cython code:

https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/blob/a3526d4f2d234b97903170f4e822f06fff80d1c1/pywt/_extensions/_dwt.pyx#L55
These types of statements occur multiple places in both `_dwt.pyx` and `_swt.pyx`.  

This means that the C code that will be generated will only include these lines if C99 support was enabled. This is problematic in terms of distributing the C code in the `sdist`: e.g. if the generated code supports C99, but the user's compiler does not!

Unless there is an alternative solution, I think this means we will need to not distribute Cython-generated C files and add Cython as a build dependency in the next release.

issue_title: re-instantiating a Connection object causes __init__ to be called again
issue_body:
collectd.Connection objects with identical parameters are singletons, so Connection() is Connection()

However, in collectd 1.0, we fail to check in our \_\_init\_\_ method whether Connection has already been initialized, and so we re-initialize all of our private data members.  This has been fixed in the repo, but will not go out to PyPI until 1.0.1

issue_title: Redesign
issue_body:
The nightly build of TF has switched to a version where we use `keras_applications` as the module `tf.keras.applications`. Unfortunately, this has created the following issue: when using Keras (not tf.keras) with the TF backend and when importing TF, the underlying Keras backend being used by keras.applications is actually tf.keras, causing this code to fail:

```python
import tensorflow as tf  # Importing tf creates the issue
import keras
xception = keras.application.Xception(weights=None)
d = keras.layers.Dense(1)(xception.outputs[0])
```

In general the fact that modules imports **represent a shared state across the entire python runtime** represents an impassable issue (e.g. we could use `reload` to fix the above, but that would introduce the reverse issue). We need to de-share our two imports of `keras_applications` somehow.

There are two possible fixes here:

- Find a way to import `keras_applications` **as a local copy** both in Keras and tf.keras. I haven't been able to get this to work (using the existing [literature](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/11170949/how-to-make-a-copy-of-a-python-module-at-runtime)). If it can be made to work, it would be a good option.
- Redefine all application signatures in Keras and tf.keras, and have the applications in `keras_applications` **receive the modules they need as keyword arguments**. In this setup the applications **make no imports from keras** (instead they get everything they need to run from their keyword arguments, which are set to correct values in the redefined signatures in the parent packages).

I will go with the second solution unless someone has a better idea.

issue_title: Build cache behavior interacts badly with unclaimed classes behavior of jvm compile
issue_body:
Current buildcache / compile behavior allows for stale class files from incremental compiles to be added to the buildcache for a newer version of a target.

Setup
target A has compile output, C, from a previous compile run with fingerprint F
target A is modified such that it matches an earlier fingerprint F1 that is cached as C1

compile target A
- hits the buildcache, sees F1
- extracts C1 into the work dir
- skips zinc compile, which causes the compile outputs C to be left in place


result
- Both C and C1 are in the work dir even though C isn't a product of the current sources
- Another change + compile will cause C to be considered 'unclaimed' classes and stuffed into the classes_by_target product and from there into the buildcache, which means the C artifacts will survive a clean-all. :(

workaround:
- turn off local build cache writing, and ensure CI jobs run clean-alls prior to interacting with new snapshots of the workspace

solutions:
- un-extract cached files if the previous fingerprint was cached when extracting a new fingerprint's cache
- change how unclaimed classes are cached, maybe ensure annotation processors changes are checked somehow, and those classes are assigned to them.
- allow tasks to specify behavior prior to extracting cached artifacts--ie isolated compile could clean out the class / analysis directories for a compilation unit prior to having the cache extracted there. 


I think the most common case is that C is actually also a cached version of A, so my initial work is toward dealing with that since that covers the case that we got a report for.

issue_title: Proper implementation of `**` globs in the v2 engine.
issue_body:
AFAICT, we don't have a proper implementation of trailing `**` matching in the new engine. This needs to be repaired, but is non-trivial.

Expectations:

  `**` -> matches everything, recursively.
  `dir/**` -> matches everything under `dir`, recursively.
  `**/*.py` -> matches every dir recursively, searching for files with a `.py` extension.

It may also make sense to define a formal spec for our globs implementation so we can code to the spec.

References:

- A spec that we are close to matching: https://git-scm.com/docs/gitignore
- A non-spec: https://github.com/bazelbuild/bazel/blob/master/src/test/java/com/google/devtools/build/lib/vfs/RecursiveGlobTest.java#L58-L76

issue_title: Hooks should have a way of specifying minimum pre-commit version to run
issue_body:
Obviously this is a bit of a chicken-and-egg problem until the feature exists, but afterwards this'll be useful.

For instance, the feature added in 0.6.6 (additional dependencies) would be useful to gate against older pre-commit versions where installation would be nonsensical.

This would be a fatal error that would require the user to upgrade pre-commit to use that hook.

I'd like to implement this first, and then change all of the mirror repositories to use the additional-deps approach.  This would alleviate the pains in #282 as well by eliminating the hacky mirroring technique that is currently required for node hooks.

issue_title: Write a hook that guards against poorly written `contextlib.contextmanager`s
issue_body:
We've seen some bugs from contextmanagers that are written like:

```
@contextlib.contextmanager
def ctx(...):
    global g
    g = 1
    yield
    g = 0
```

Where they should have `try: yield finally:` to be exception-safe.

Without thinking too much about it I think we can use this heuristic:

- functions which are generators and are decorated by contextmanager or contextlib.contextmanager should either have a finally block or themselves contain a with statement

issue_title: How to catch exception inside of coroutine that was created by asyncio.ensure_future
issue_body:
I have scenario that tests part of app with a complex schedule of concurrent tasks where I'm using `asyncio.ensure_future`. Here is simplified example of it:
```python 
import asyncio
import pytest

async def a_second():
    await asyncio.sleep(0.1)

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_very_lazy_task(event_loop):
    async def one():
        # we are waiting for this exception which isn't never caught
        pytest.fail('Got it!')

    # param loop=event_loop doesn't influent on a result at all
    asyncio.ensure_future(one(), loop=event_loop)
    await a_second()
    # with this guy we can check that we actually have Got it error in our logs
    # pytest.fail('after all!')
```
Sadly it seems that function `one` falls out of pytest asyncio sphere of influence. And pytest.fail('Got it!') raises exception but was never caught by pytest.

with `pytest.fail('after all!')` I got:

________________________________________ test_very_lazy_task ________________________________________

event_loop = <_UnixSelectorEventLoop running=False closed=True debug=False>

    @pytest.mark.asyncio
    async def test_very_lazy_task(event_loop):
        async def one():
            pytest.fail('Got it!')

        asyncio.ensure_future(one(), loop=event_loop)
        await a_second()
>       pytest.fail('after all!')
E       Failed: after all!

fb/conversation/test_story.py:125: Failed
--------------------------------------- Captured stderr setup ---------------------------------------
DEBUG:asyncio:Using selector: EpollSelector
---------------------------------------- Captured log setup -----------------------------------------
selector_events.py          53 DEBUG    Using selector: EpollSelector
--------------------------------------- Captured stderr call ----------------------------------------
ERROR:asyncio:Task exception was never retrieved
future: <Task finished coro=<test_very_lazy_task.<locals>.one() done, defined at /usr/src/app/fb/conversation/test_story.py:120> exception=Got it!>
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/asyncio/tasks.py", line 239, in _step
    result = coro.send(None)
  File "/usr/src/app/fb/conversation/test_story.py", line 121, in one
    pytest.fail('Got it!')
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/_pytest/runner.py", line 486, in fail
    raise Failed(msg=msg, pytrace=pytrace)
Failed: Got it!
----------------------------------------- Captured log call -----------------------------------------
base_events.py            1090 ERROR    Task exception was never retrieved
future: <Task finished coro=<test_very_lazy_task.<locals>.one() done, defined at /usr/src/app/fb/conversation/test_story.py:120> exception=Got it!>
Traceback (most recent call last):

So what it the right way to catch exception inside of coroutine functions that were launched  by asyncio.ensure_future?

issue_title: Unexpected categorizer output regarding underscores
issue_body:

The following script reproduces:
```py
import tensorflow as tf

def main():
  k = tf.constant(0)
  tf.summary.scalar('loss', k)
  tf.summary.scalar('loss/regularization', k)
  tf.summary.scalar('loss_extra_stuff', k)
  summ = tf.summary.merge_all()

  sess = tf.Session()
  writer = tf.summary.FileWriter('/tmp/cat2')
  writer.add_graph(sess.graph)
  writer.add_summary(sess.run(summ))
  writer.close()

if __name__ == '__main__':
  main()
```

In TensorBoard, the categorizer emits two categories called "loss", one with tag "loss" and one with "loss/regularization". This should not happen. It does not occur if the third summary is removed.

issue_title: Tensorflow and tensorboard depend on each other
issue_body:
Currently tensorflow and tensorboard depend on each other:
tensorflow(1.3.0) needs: tensorflow-tensorboard (<0.2.0,>=0.1.0)
tensorflow-tensorboard(0.1.4) needs: tensorflow (>=1.2.0)

And also tensorflow-gpu has a dependency on tensorboard:
tensorflow(1.3.0) needs: tensorflow-tensorboard (<0.2.0,>=0.1.0)

This result a problem:
If I install tensorflow-gpu from ``pip install tensorflow-gpu`` , it will install
tensorboard dependency and then install tensorflow from tensorboard 
inpendency.  And then tensorflow package overrides the gpu package.

Are there any ways to solve this?
Should the mutual dependency be decoupled?

issue_title: Resizing the debugger plugin does not work at non-default zoom levels.
issue_body:
To repro, navigate to the debugger dashboard in Chrome. Either zoom in or out via Chrome. Resize the left pane. Notice that the mouse will increase in distance (depart) from the resizer as the user drags left or right.

This is because the debugger resizer logic obtains the mouse X position using `event.screenX` during drags. The `screenX` property returns the screen position in device pixels. We should instead use CSS pixels because we resize by updating the CSS width of the left pane.

issue_title: Feature request: A class name for missing icons
issue_body: ### Describe the problem you'd like to see solved or task you'd like to see made easier I wish to hide the following missing identifier: ![missing icon animation](https://i.imgur.com/wlygrFk.gif) My simplest solution for it (using the SVG with JS framework) is to add a class, `fa-missing-icon` or something, when the icon is missing. ### Is this in relation to an existing part of Font Awesome or something new? It supposed to be really simple and obvious, as the function `findIcon()` already throws an exception for missing icons. ### What is 1 thing that we can do when building this feature that will guarantee that it is awesome? Only add the class name to the icon, so I could target it through CSS and apply `display: none;` on it. ### Why would other Font Awesome users care about this? I let users on my website choose their own representing icon, and they may choose a brand icon which uses a different prefix than normal icons. My plan is to generate two icons with the following template: `$custom_icon_template_1 = "";` `$custom_icon_template_2 = "";` and then apply `display: none;` to the missing icon. That way there will always be a present icon. ### On a scale of 1 (sometime in the future) to 10 (absolutely right now), how soon would you recommend we make this feature? 10. I think this feature is too simple to postpone, but it's just me 😉 ### Feature request checklist - [ V ] This is a single feature. - [ V ] The title starts with "Feature request: " and is followed by a clear feature name. - [ V ] I have [searched for existing issues](https://github.com/FortAwesome/Font-Awesome-Pro/issues) and to the best of my knowledge this is not a duplicate.

issue_title: Memory leak using websocket protocol
issue_body:
I encountered a memory leak using the websocket protocol `ws://` in the `WebSocketsReader.read()` method because the method `self._protocol.recv()` is blocking until a websocket message is received.
I believe this websockets library should be used with a single recv loop, otherwise you might end up stacking coroutines blocking at `recv`.
I was not able to fix it right now but the problem is definitely there. Any chance you could look into it ?

issue_title: macOS + FreeTDS + MSSQL: cannot execute with params
issue_body:
On macOS + FreeTDS + MSSQL database I cannot execute an INSERT statement with parameters. I get the following error:
```
DatabaseError: ODBC error
state: IM001
native error code: 0
message: [unixODBC][Driver Manager]Driver does not support this function
```
It seems like the message is truncated: I think it should show what ODBC function is being called.

To reproduce:
```python
conn = turbodbc.connect(dsn='mssql', uid='test', pwd='test')
cursor = conn.cursor()
cursor.execute("CREATE TABLE test_insert (val INT);")
cursor.execute("INSERT INTO test_insert (val) VALUES (?);", (0,))
```

Doing an insert without parameterization works fine, e.g.
`cursor.execute("INSERT INTO test_insert (val) VALUES (42);")`
works fine.

issue_title: Python 2.7 Windows Support
issue_body:
Hello,

I stumbled across this while looking for an alternative to pyodbc for an ETL application that I've built using pandas and sqlalchemy. Because of existing design decisions and my organization's internal IT structure, I'm somewhat stuck using Windows Server and MS SQL Server for hosting the app and data that I'm processing. The current application is built in Python 2.7 and everything works great except for very  slow insert times with pyodbc for large (10,000 - 400,000) datasets into MSSQL. I have adapted a "fast_load" workstream with ceODBC, but it doesn't seem to be actively developed and some of my data sets have NVARCHAR('max') fields that throw an error when the values are too large (doesn't happen with pyodbc).

Long story short, I'd like to replace them both with one module that is fast and can handle the large strings. Am I in the right place?

I saw that Windows support was recently released for Python 3.5+. Is there any chance of getting Python 2.7 support also? I'd be willing to help implement the necessary changes to downgrade, but I thought I'd see what the general thoughts on the level of difficulty is first.

issue_title: Color code error messages

issue_title: Client functionality to proceed with partially-authorized cert requests
issue_body:
For renewal, we may want to renew even when only a subset of the subject names in an existing cert can be renewed successfully.

In the normal certificate issuance case, we abandon the attempt to obtain the cert if the CA tells us that we failed to obtain authorization for any requested subject name. But for renewal, this might not be our preferred behavior. Instead, we may want to be able to proceed with requesting issuance for the successfully-authorized subset of subject names.

In this case we need a way to specify this behavior and to report back to the caller to indicate which names were successfully issued for and which names couldn't be issued for (and, if the information is available, why not).

2. ) Right now when an invalid email address is given, the error message says 'an unexpected error occured, please check the logfile'. This should be changed to something along the lines of "You gave an invalid email address. Please enter a valid email address for recovery purposes and notifications, or leave the field empty to give no email address".

issue_title: Maybe make task-local storage work from run_in_worker_thread functions
issue_body:
This is handy for logging, and safe because we know that *either* the task *or* the thread is running, but never both - so no locking is needed. But maybe confusing (it can't work for run-locals, or `run_in_worker_process`)?

What should `current_task()` return in a `run_in_worker_thread` function? Currently we effectively find the task-locals by looking up the current task, so the most naive way to make task-locals work would to make `current_task()` work and then task-locals would work for free. Is that good or bad?

issue_title: Ray serialization fails on subtypes of dictionaries.
issue_body:
If we serialize and deserialize a `defaultdict`, we will get back a regular dictionary.

```python
from collections import defaultdict
import ray

ray.init(num_cpus=0)

d = defaultdict(lambda: 0)
print(type(d))

result = ray.get(ray.put(d))
print(type(result))
```

The output is

```
<class 'collections.defaultdict'>
<class 'dict'>
```

issue_title: OSError: FIle name too long
issue_body:
### System information
- **OS Platform and Distribution (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 16.04)**: Linux Ubuntu 16.04

- **Ray version**: 0.3.0

issue_title: Automatic extras on nested inlines causes field is required error
issue_body:
After playing around a bit, I noticed that if I create a top level inline and it has nested inlines, if the nested inlines have an extras value > 0, the extra inlines that are automatically there won't save the first time. All of their fields will be cleared and the "Field Is Required" error will show for all of the fields that are required on those extra inlines.

I was able to work around this by simply setting each inline to use have 0 extras and then just using the "add <inline>" buttons to add more. If my explanation wasn't clear enough, I can post a video of what is happening.

issue_title: Add support for installing manticore with newest capstone
issue_body:
Some "bugs" in Manticore are actually just bugs in capstone (e.g. #560). We've historically just added various hacks in the codebase to hack around these bugs (e.g. #852), since there is no pypi release for newer capstone versions where these are fixed (v4+).

We should investigate using the setup.py dependency links feature to allow users to install a capstone version from a github link (see https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/pull/852#pullrequestreview-108970169) if they opt in using the `--process-dependency-links`. the desired behavior

- if a user does `pip install manticore` they get manticore installed with latest capstone on pypi
- if a user does `pip install --process-dependency-links manticore` they get manticore installed with the latest capstone `next` branch 

help would be greatly appreciated with this!

what needs to be done:
- something along the lines of setup.py should be modified to use the dependnecy links feature linking to https://github.com/aquynh/capstone/archive/next.zip#subdirectory=bindings/python
- test installing via `pip install .` -> confirm the capstone version is 3.0.5rc2
- test installing via `pip install --process-dependency-links .` -> confirm the capstone version is 4+
- modify the readme to document that the `--process-dependency-links` argument may be used by binary analysis users who are experiencing error and unexpected "invalid memory access" testcases

--

thanks to @disconnect3d for the suggestion!

issue_title: field larger than field limit (131072)
issue_body:
When running the command `csvsql --db postgresql://postgres@localhost/archi_db_dev --no-create --insert pc_fr.csv --db-schema lookup --table postcode`, I get the error message `field larger than field limit (131072)`.

The schema and csv file are attached in [test.zip](https://github.com/wireservice/csvkit/files/865198/test.zip).

I've analysed the csv file, but all columns are within the limits of the pre-created table. Also, the bug seems a bit random, because yesterday I got the error message on another csv file (postal codes of another province). Yesterday, the bug happened multiple times with the other datafile, today it loads fine. But now the included file doesn't want to load (both when the table is truncated and recreated).

Questions:

issue_title: [V3] Enforcing use of our Command objects
issue_body:
As we continue to modify the Command objects in `redbot.core.commands` to tailor the library more towards Red, we create more features which are dependent on the modified behaviour. This has become more apparent with fuzzy help search (which relies on a new method `Command.parents` - normal `discord.ext.commands.Command` objects won't appear in fuzzy help) and also possible future changes to the `Permissions` cog, in order to fix some logic errors and enhance performance overall.

All we ask of cog creators is to replace the line `from discord.ext import commands` or equivalent with `from redbot.core import commands`. In exchange, we have more freedom in the features we can add to command objects in order to enhance core features.

Edit: 

If you are adamant on maintaining compatibility with other non-red frameworks, it is possible to do a conditional import:
```py
try:
    from redbot.core import commands
except ImportError:
    from discord.ext import commands
```

Any queries, qualms or suggestions, please comment below.

issue_title: [V3] Repo's not showing up after restart
issue_body:
# Other bugs


#### What were you trying to do?

When I add a repo through the command to my bot it goes into the RepoManager and in repos.json as it should.

#### What were you expecting to happen?

I was expecting it to go into the [p]repo list command which is does but temporarily until the bot restarts

#### What actually happened?

It only shows the repos I add on that restart so if I add 3 repo's and restart the bot they all disappear from [p]repo list. However their files are still in RepoManager and their details are still in Repo.json

#### How can we reproduce this issue?

For me to reproduce this issue I installed a fresh bot on a new machine, added a couple of repositories and then restarted the bot. I then ran [p]repo list and they were all gone from the list.

issue_title: Provide State Machine Information via API
issue_body: When ExaBGP is used to generate MRT file from a received routing table, the MRT dump need to include BGP FSM code. It would simplify the code of the backend process if ExaBGP were to generate this data.

issue_title: Global disabling of all graphics
issue_body:
One of the issues I found when testing on Travis ( #37), and also when running Netpyne scripts remotely was that matplotlib was required, and that some graphical elements were initialised leading to errors related to no display for windows.

It would be great to be able to set one global variable in netpyne to say: ignore all plotting etc. *and don't even try to import matplotlib etc*. This would allow scripts such as this:  https://github.com/Neurosim-lab/netpyne/blob/master/examples/HHTut/HHTut_run.py to search for a command line arg (-nogui) and set that one global variable in netpyne, as opposed to individually disabling gui elements, e.g. https://github.com/OpenSourceBrain/NetPyNEShowcase/blob/master/NetPyNE/HHSmall/HH_run.py

issue_body:
#------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Network clamp (integrate into netpyne?)
#------------------------------------------------------------------------------
if cfg.netClamp:
	def ijson_load(filename, gid, loadTags=True, loadConns=True):
	    #import ijson
	    import ijson.backends.yajl2_cffi as ijson
	    from time import time
	    tags, conns = [], None

	    print 'Reading data'
	    with open(filename, 'r') as fd:
	        start = time()
	        objs = ijson.items(fd, 'net.cells.item')
	        if loadTags and loadConns:
	        	for cell in objs:
	        		tags.append(cell['tags'])
	        		if cell['gid'] == gid:
	        			conns = cell['conns']
	        elif loadTags:
	            tags = [cell['tags'] for cell in objs]
	        elif loadConns:                
	            conns = next((cell['conns'] for cell in objs if cell['gid']==gid),[])

	        print 'time ellapsed (s): ', time() - start
	    return tags, conns

	# load conns of cells
	loadFull = 0
	path = cfg.saveFolder

	if loadFull:
	    tags,conns = ijson_load(cfg.netClampConnsFile, cfg.netClampGid, loadTags=False)
	    if tags:
	        with open(path+'/netClamp_tags.pkl', 'w') as fileObj: pickle.dump({'tags': tags}, fileObj) 
	    else:
	        with open(path+'/netClamp_tags.pkl', 'r') as fileObj: tags = pickle.load(fileObj)['tags']
	    if conns:
	        with open(path+'/netClamp_conns.pkl', 'w') as fileObj: pickle.dump({'conns': conns}, fileObj)
	    else:
	        with open(path+'/netClamp_conns.pkl', 'r') as fileObj: conns = pickle.load(fileObj)['conns']
	else:
	    with open(path+'/netClamp_tags.pkl', 'r') as fileObj: tags = pickle.load(fileObj)['tags']
	    with open(path+'/netClamp_conns.pkl', 'r') as fileObj: conns = pickle.load(fileObj)['conns']


	# load list of preGid cell
	preGids = list(set([conn['preGid'] for conn in conns]))

	# load spk times of preGid cells
	with open(cfg.netClampSpikesFile, 'r') as fileObj: data = json.load(fileObj)
	allSpkids,allSpkts = data['simData']['spkid'], data['simData']['spkt']
	spkids,spkts,spkpops = zip(*[(spkid,spkt,tags[int(spkid)]['popLabel']) for spkid,spkt in zip(allSpkids,allSpkts) if spkid in preGids])

	# group by prepops
	prePops = list(set(spkpops))

	# make weightNorm = 1 !! -- otherwise twice

	# create one vectsim pop reproducing spk times per preGid; and multiple conns 
	for prePop in prePops:
	    # get spkTimes for preGid
	    spkGids = [spkid for spkid,spkpop in zip(spkids,spkpops) if spkpop == prePop]

	    if len(spkGids) > 0:
	        # get list of cell gids in this pop 
	        popGids = list(set(spkGids))

	        # set key/label of pop
	        key = '' + str(prePop)

	        # create 1 vectstim pop per conn (cellLabel=cell gid -- so can reference later)
	        cellsList = []
	        for popGid in popGids: 
	            spkTimes = [spkt for spkid,spkt in zip(spkids,spkts) if spkid == popGid]
	            if len(spkTimes) > 0:
	                cellsList.append({'cellLabel': int(popGid), 'spkTimes': spkTimes})
	                netParams.popParams[key] = {'cellModel': 'VecStim', 'cellsList': cellsList}

	                # calculate conns for this preGid
	                preConns = [conn for conn in conns if conn['preGid'] == popGid]

	                for i,preConn in enumerate(preConns):
	                    # connect vecstim to cell
	                    netParams.connParams[key+'_'+str(popGid)+'_'+str(i)] = { 
	                            'preConds': {'cellLabel': preConn['preGid']},  # cellLabel corresponds to gid 
	                            'postConds': {'popLabel': cfg.netClampPop},
	                            'synMech': str(preConn['synMech']),
	                            'weight': float(preConn['weight']), 
	                            'delay': float(preConn['delay']),
	                            'sec': str(preConn['sec']), 
	                            'loc': str(preConn['loc'])}

issue_title: pyqode: add support for FIXME/TODO markers
issue_body:
Looks like a pyqode.core feature (I wasn't able to create an issue there - and would like to see the backport in OCIDE, too, therefore creating it here, feel free to copy/move this issue around [for example the pyqode-part there, only a minor part to OCIDE and the full experience to HE]):

The following would have to be done:

* define a format how todo/to-do/fixme markers look like (ideally: use a standard formatting and let the user add his own via IDE options, *maybe* with a severity (1-3, something like `*> IDEA:`, `*> TODO:`, *> FIXME:`)
* check for these markers
* show them in the IDE

For the IDE options:
* option to define these markers and their severity, maybe per-file-type (not per file-extension)
* maybe give options to show them only from a given severity
* show these markers in the source view
* show these in the "lower pane" next to the issues - or include them there (at least for the severity 2+3)
